question: What is a closure? Give a real use.
answer:
A closure is a function that keeps access to variables from its outer (lexical) scope even after the outer function has returned. The inner function "remembers" the environment it was created in.

Uses: data privacy (private variables), function factories, and holding state in callbacks — e.g. a debounce keeps its timer id in a closure.
tip: Closures are the answer to 'how do you make a private variable in JavaScript'.
deep:
The definition, precisely

A closure is the combination of a function and the lexical environment it was defined in. When you create a function inside another function, the inner one keeps a live reference to the outer one's variables — even after the outer function has returned and its call is gone from the stack.

Why it works

Scope in JS is lexical (decided by where code is written, not how it's called). The inner function's scope chain still points at the outer variables, so the garbage collector keeps them alive as long as the closure exists.

function makeCounter() {
  let n = 0;              // private, lives on
  return {
    inc() { return ++n; },
    reset() { n = 0; }
  };
}
const c = makeCounter();
c.inc(); // 1
c.inc(); // 2   — n is shared & hidden

Real-world uses

  • Data privacy / encapsulation — n above can't be touched from outside.
  • Function factories — const add5 = makeAdder(5).
  • Stateful callbacks — debounce/throttle keep their timer id in a closure.
  • Memoization — cache stored in the enclosing scope.
  • Event handlers — remember data from when they were attached.
function makeAdder(x) { return y => x + y; }
const add5 = makeAdder(5);
add5(2); // 7

The classic gotcha (loop + var)

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0); // 3, 3, 3
}
// fix: use let (new binding per iteration) → 0, 1, 2

All three callbacks close over the same var i; by the time they run, the loop finished and i is 3. let creates a fresh binding each iteration.

How to say it in an interview

“A closure is a function that remembers the variables from the scope where it was created, even after that scope has exited. It’s how JavaScript does private state — I use it for factories, memoization, and keeping state like a debounce timer.”

question: var vs let vs const, and what is hoisting?
answer:
• var — function-scoped, hoisted and initialised to undefined, re-declarable.
  • let — block-scoped, hoisted but in the Temporal Dead Zone (error if used before declaration).
  • const — block-scoped, assigned once. The binding is constant, not the object — you can still mutate a const array/object.

Hoisting: declarations are moved to the top of their scope at compile time. var and function declarations are usable early; let/const are not.
tip: Default to const, use let when you must reassign, avoid var.
deep:
Side-by-side

varletconst
Scopefunctionblockblock
Hoistedyes → undefinedyes → TDZyes → TDZ
Re-declareallowednono
Re-assignyesyesno
Global propadds to windownono

What hoisting actually is

At compile time the engine scans a scope and registers its declarations before running any line. So the declaration is “moved up,” but the assignment stays put.

console.log(a); // undefined (declaration hoisted, value not)
var a = 5;

console.log(b); // ReferenceError (TDZ)
let b = 5;

var is initialised to undefined when hoisted; let/const are hoisted but left uninitialised — reading them before the declaration line throws (the Temporal Dead Zone).

Block scope matters

if (true) { var x = 1; let y = 2; }
console.log(x); // 1  — var leaks out of the block
console.log(y); // ReferenceError — let is block-scoped

const is not "immutable"

const freezes the binding, not the value. You can still mutate the contents of a const object/array — use Object.freeze() for shallow immutability.

const arr = [1];
arr.push(2);   // ✅ allowed — same reference
arr = [];      // ❌ TypeError — reassigning the binding

Function declarations vs expressions

Function declarations are fully hoisted (callable before their line). Function expressions assigned to var/let follow the variable's hoisting rules.

How to say it in an interview

“Use const by default, let when you must reassign, and avoid var because it’s function-scoped and leaks. Hoisting means declarations are processed before execution — var becomes undefined, while let/const sit in the TDZ and throw if used early.”

question: Explain the event loop, microtasks vs macrotasks.
answer:
JS is single-threaded. The call stack runs synchronous code. Async callbacks wait in queues and the event loop pushes them onto the stack when it's empty.

  • Microtask queue — Promise .then, queueMicrotask, await continuations. Fully drained after each task.
  • Macrotask queue — setTimeout, setInterval, I/O, UI events.

Microtasks always run before the next macrotask.
tip: Classic trick question: a resolved Promise's .then logs before a setTimeout(…, 0).
deep:
The pieces

  • Call stack — where synchronous code runs, one frame at a time.
  • Web/Node APIs — timers, fetch, DOM events run outside the engine and hand back callbacks when done.
  • Macrotask (task) queue — setTimeout, setInterval, I/O, UI events.
  • Microtask queue — Promise .then/.catch/.finally, await continuations, queueMicrotask.

The golden rule

After each macrotask (and after the initial synchronous run), the engine drains the entire microtask queue before rendering or picking up the next macrotask. Microtasks always beat macrotasks.

console.log('A');
setTimeout(() => console.log('B'), 0);   // macrotask
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('C')); // microtask
console.log('D');
// A, D, C, B

Order: sync A, D run first; the stack empties; microtask C drains before the timer; macrotask B last.

Microtasks can starve the queue

Promise.resolve().then(function loop() {
  Promise.resolve().then(loop); // keeps adding microtasks
});
setTimeout(() => console.log('never soon'), 0);

Because the microtask queue must fully drain first, an endless chain of microtasks blocks timers and rendering.

await is just Promise scheduling

async function f() {
  console.log(1);
  await null;          // pause; rest becomes a microtask
  console.log(3);
}
console.log(0); f(); console.log(2);
// 0, 1, 2, 3

Everything after an await is scheduled as a microtask — the synchronous code around the call runs first.

How to say it in an interview

“JS runs on one thread with a call stack. Async callbacks wait in queues; the event loop moves them onto the stack when it’s empty. Microtasks — Promise callbacks and await continuations — are fully drained after each task, so a resolved Promise’s .then always runs before a setTimeout(0).”

question: == vs === ?
answer:
== compares after type coercion (0 == "0" is true). === checks value and type with no coercion.

Always use === unless you deliberately want coercion (e.g. x == null to catch both null and undefined).
tip: x == null is the one idiomatic use of == — it matches null and undefined.
code:
0 == '';    // true
0 === '';   // false
null == undefined; // true

question: How does the this keyword work?
answer:
this depends on how a function is called:

  • Regular call → global object, or undefined in strict mode.
  • Method call obj.fn() → obj.
  • Arrow function → no own this; inherits from the enclosing scope.
  • call / apply / bind → set this explicitly.
tip: Arrow functions are why callbacks 'just work' inside classes — they keep the outer this.
deep:
The one rule: it's decided at call time

For a normal function, this is not set by where it's defined — it's set by how it's called. Work through these binding rules in order:

Call formthis is
new Fn()the brand-new object
fn.call/apply/bind(o)o (explicit)
obj.fn()obj (the receiver)
fn() aloneundefined (strict) / global (sloppy)
arrow functioninherited from enclosing scope

The "lost this" bug

const obj = {
  name: 'A',
  greet() { return this.name; }
};
const g = obj.greet;
g();          // undefined — called plain, no receiver
obj.greet();  // 'A'

Detaching a method drops its receiver. Fixes: obj.greet.bind(obj), wrap in an arrow, or define the method as a class field arrow.

Why arrow functions fix callbacks

Arrows have no own this — they capture it lexically from where they're written. That's why a setTimeout or array callback inside a method keeps the right this:

class Timer {
  seconds = 0;
  start() { setInterval(() => this.seconds++, 1000); } // arrow keeps `this`
}

A normal function() { this.seconds++ } here would break — its this would be the timer/global, not the instance.

Don't use arrows for object methods

const o = { v: 1, get() => this.v };
o.get(); // undefined — arrow's this is the outer scope, not o

How to say it in an interview

“this depends on how a function is called, not where it’s defined: new → the new object, call/apply/bind → explicit, obj.fn() → the object, a bare call → undefined in strict mode. Arrow functions have no own this; they inherit it — which is exactly why they’re great for callbacks and wrong for object methods.”

question: Promise vs async/await, and how do you handle errors?
answer:
A Promise represents a future value (pending → fulfilled/rejected). async/await is syntactic sugar that lets asynchronous code read synchronously.

Errors: .catch() on a chain, or try/catch around await. Use Promise.all for parallel and Promise.allSettled when you need every result regardless of failures.
tip: await inside a loop is sequential — use Promise.all to parallelise independent calls.
deep:
A Promise's three states

pending → then either fulfilled (has a value) or rejected (has a reason). It settles once and never changes again.

async/await is sugar over Promises

An async function always returns a Promise. await pauses the function until the awaited Promise settles, unwrapping the value (or throwing the rejection). It reads top-to-bottom but is still non-blocking.

// chain
fetch('/user').then(r => r.json()).then(use).catch(handle);
// same thing, flatter
async function load() {
  try { const r = await fetch('/user'); use(await r.json()); }
  catch (e) { handle(e); }
}

Error handling — the four traps

  • try/catch around await, or .catch() on a chain.
  • fetch doesn't reject on HTTP errors — a 404/500 still fulfils. Check res.ok yourself.
  • A missing catch → an unhandled promise rejection.
  • Forgetting await → you handle the Promise object, not the value, and the try/catch won't catch its rejection.
const res = await fetch(url);
if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${res.status}`);

Sequential vs parallel

// ❌ sequential — waits ~2x
const a = await getA();
const b = await getB();

// ✅ parallel — independent calls at once
const [a, b] = await Promise.all([getA(), getB()]);

await inside a for loop runs one at a time. For independent work, kick them all off and Promise.all — or allSettled when you need every result even if some fail.

How to say it in an interview

“A Promise is a future value; async/await is syntax sugar that makes it read synchronously. I handle errors with try/catch around await — remembering fetch doesn’t reject on 4xx/5xx so I check res.ok — and I use Promise.all to parallelise independent calls instead of awaiting them in sequence.”

question: Debounce vs throttle — what and when?
answer:
Debounce delays execution until events stop firing for a specified time. If another event occurs before the delay ends, the timer resets. It is ideal for search boxes, autocomplete, and validation.

Throttle limits execution to at most once every specified interval, regardless of how many events occur. It is ideal for scroll, resize, drag, and mouse movement events.
tip: Think: Debounce = Wait until the user stops. Throttle = Execute at a fixed rate.
deep:
Why do we need Debounce and Throttle?

Browser events like scroll, mousemove, resize, and keyup can fire hundreds of times per second. Executing expensive logic for every event can slow down the application and increase unnecessary API calls.

Debounce

Debounce waits until the user stops triggering events for a specified duration before executing the function. Every new event resets the timer.

User typing:
H
He
Hel
Hell
Hello

Timer resets each time

After user stops typing (300ms)
        ↓
API Call

Real-world uses:

  • Search autocomplete
  • Username availability check
  • Form validation
  • Saving drafts after typing stops
searchInput.addEventListener(
  'input',
  debounce(() => {
    searchProducts();
  }, 300)
);

Throttle

Throttle ensures a function executes at most once during a specified interval, even if events continue firing.

Scroll Events
||||||||||||||||||||||

Throttle (500ms)
↓       ↓       ↓
Run     Run     Run

Real-world uses:

  • Scroll events
  • Window resize
  • Mouse movement
  • Infinite scrolling
  • Drag & Drop
window.addEventListener(
  'scroll',
  throttle(() => {
    updateProgressBar();
  }, 200)
);

Comparison

FeatureDebounceThrottle
ExecutionAfter events stopAt fixed intervals
Best ForSearch, ValidationScroll, Resize, Mouse Move
API CallsUsually OneMultiple (Rate Limited)
GoalAvoid unnecessary executionsLimit execution frequency

Angular Example

RxJS provides built-in operators for these patterns.

// Debounce
searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300)
);

// Throttle
fromEvent(window, 'scroll').pipe(
  throttleTime(200)
);

Interview Tip

If asked which one to use for a search bar, answer Debounce because you only want to call the API after the user finishes typing. If asked about scroll or resize events, answer Throttle because you still want periodic updates while the event continues.

Interview Answer

"Debounce delays execution until events stop occurring, making it ideal for search boxes and form validation. Throttle limits execution to once every specified interval, making it ideal for continuous events like scrolling, resizing, and mouse movement. Debounce reduces unnecessary work, while throttle controls the execution rate."

question: map vs forEach vs reduce.
answer: map returns a new transformed array. forEach just iterates and returns nothing. reduce folds an array into a single value (sum, group-by, flatten, etc.).
tip: Use map when you need a result; forEach only for side effects.

question: Shallow copy vs deep copy.
answer:
Shallow copy creates a new object but copies only the first level. Nested objects and arrays are still shared references, so modifying them affects both the original and the copied object.

Deep copy creates a completely independent clone, including all nested objects and arrays. Modern JavaScript provides structuredClone() for deep cloning. The older JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()) approach works only for simple JSON data and loses Dates, Maps, Sets, functions, undefined values, and class instances.
tip: Remember: Spread operator creates a shallow copy. For true deep cloning, prefer structuredClone().
deep:
Why do we need Copying?

Objects and arrays in JavaScript are reference types. Assigning one object to another variable copies only the reference, not the actual object.

const user1 = { name: 'John' };
const user2 = user1;

user2.name = 'David';

console.log(user1.name); // David

Both variables point to the same object in memory.

Shallow Copy

A shallow copy creates a new object but copies only the first level. Nested objects are still shared.

const original = {
  name: 'John',
  address: {
    city: 'Bangalore'
  }
};

const copy = { ...original };

copy.address.city = 'Mumbai';

console.log(original.address.city); // Mumbai
Memory

original ─────┐
              │
              ▼
         address {}
              ▲
              │
copy ─────────┘

The outer object is new, but the nested address object is shared.

Ways to Create a Shallow Copy

  • { ...obj }
  • Object.assign({}, obj)
  • array.slice()
  • Array.from()

Deep Copy

A deep copy recursively clones every nested object and array, producing a completely independent object.

const deep = structuredClone(original);

deep.address.city = 'Delhi';

console.log(original.address.city); // Bangalore
Memory

original ──► address {}

deep ─────► address {}

Each object has its own nested data.

structuredClone()

structuredClone() is the recommended modern API for deep cloning. It supports nested objects, arrays, Dates, Maps, Sets, ArrayBuffers, and many other built-in types.

const clone = structuredClone(original);

JSON.parse(JSON.stringify())

This older technique performs a deep copy only for JSON-compatible data.

const clone = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(original));

Limitations:

  • ❌ Removes functions.
  • ❌ Removes undefined values.
  • ❌ Converts Date to string.
  • ❌ Loses Map and Set.
  • ❌ Cannot clone circular references.
  • ❌ Loses class instances and prototypes.

Comparison

FeatureShallow CopyDeep Copy
Top-level propertiesCopiedCopied
Nested objectsSharedIndependent
PerformanceFastSlower
Memory UsageLowHigher
Best ForSimple objectsNested structures

Real-world Example

NgRx and Redux require immutable state updates.

// ❌ Wrong
state.user.name = 'John';

// ✅ Correct
return {
  ...state,
  user: {
    ...state.user,
    name: 'John'
  }
};

Using shallow copies of each changed level preserves immutability while avoiding unnecessary deep cloning.

Interview Tip

Interviewers often ask whether the spread operator performs a deep copy. The answer is No. It creates only a shallow copy. Mention that structuredClone() is the preferred modern solution for deep cloning, but in frameworks like Angular and React, we usually avoid deep cloning entire objects for performance reasons and instead update only the necessary nested properties immutably.

Interview Answer

"A shallow copy duplicates only the first level of an object, so nested objects remain shared references. A deep copy recursively clones all nested objects, making the clone completely independent. The spread operator and Object.assign() create shallow copies, while structuredClone() performs a true deep copy. In Angular and NgRx, we typically use shallow copies with immutable updates instead of deep cloning entire state objects for better performance."

question: Explain prototypal inheritance.
answer: Prototypal inheritance is JavaScript's way of sharing properties and methods between objects. Every object has a hidden link called a [[Prototype]]. If a property or method is not found on an object, JavaScript automatically looks for it in its prototype, then continues searching up the prototype chain until it finds it or reaches null.
tip: Remember: JavaScript objects inherit from other objects, not classes. The class keyword is just a cleaner syntax built on top of prototypes.
deep:
What is Prototypal Inheritance?

In JavaScript, every object can inherit properties and methods from another object. This mechanism is called prototypal inheritance.

Instead of copying methods into every object, JavaScript shares them through a prototype chain.

How does it work?

When you access a property, JavaScript follows these steps:

  1. Look for the property on the object itself.
  2. If not found, look in its prototype.
  3. Continue searching up the prototype chain.
  4. Stop when the property is found or when the prototype becomes null.

Example

const animal = {
  speak() {
    return 'Animal sound';
  }
};

const dog = Object.create(animal);

dog.bark = function () {
  return 'Woof!';
};

console.log(dog.bark());
// Woof!

console.log(dog.speak());
// Animal sound

dog doesn't have a speak() method, so JavaScript finds it on animal.

The Prototype Chain

dog
  ↓
animal
  ↓
Object.prototype
  ↓
null

JavaScript searches each object in this chain until it finds the requested property.

How do JavaScript Classes work?

ES6 class syntax is simply a cleaner way to work with prototypes.

class Animal {
  speak() {
    return 'Animal sound';
  }
}

class Dog extends Animal {}

const dog = new Dog();

console.log(dog.speak());

Although this looks like class-based inheritance, JavaScript is still using the prototype chain behind the scenes.

Why use prototypes?

Methods are stored once on the prototype instead of being copied into every object, which saves memory.

class Person {
  greet() {
    console.log('Hello');
  }
}

const p1 = new Person();
const p2 = new Person();

Both p1 and p2 share the same greet() method.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is the prototype chain? The chain JavaScript follows when looking for a property.
  • Do JavaScript classes use prototypes? Yes. Classes are syntax sugar over prototypes.
  • Why are methods stored on the prototype? To avoid creating duplicate copies for every object.
  • How can you create an object with a custom prototype? Using Object.create().

How to say it in an interview

"JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance, where every object has a hidden prototype reference. If a property isn't found on an object, JavaScript searches its prototype, then continues up the prototype chain until the property is found or the chain ends. ES6 classes don't change this behavior—they simply provide a cleaner syntax on top of prototypes."

question: What is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)?
answer: The span between entering a scope and the point where a let/const variable is declared. The binding is hoisted but not initialised, so accessing it in that window throws a ReferenceError.
tip: TDZ is why let/const 'feel' un-hoisted even though they technically are hoisted.
code:
console.log(x); // ReferenceError (TDZ)
let x = 5;
deep:
What it is

The TDZ is the region of a scope from its start up to the line that declares a let/const (or class). The binding exists (it was hoisted) but is uninitialised, so any read or write in that window throws a ReferenceError.

{
  // TDZ for x starts here
  console.log(x); // ❌ ReferenceError
  let x = 5;      // TDZ ends — x initialised
  console.log(x); // ✅ 5
}

Hoisted but not initialised

People say let/const "aren't hoisted" — technically they are, but unlike var (which is pre-set to undefined) they get no value until execution reaches the declaration. The TDZ is what enforces that.

Why it exists

It turns "using a variable before you declare it" into a loud error instead of a silent undefined — catching real bugs, and making const meaningful (a const can't be observed before it's assigned).

A subtle trap

let y = 1;
function f() {
  console.log(y); // ❌ ReferenceError, not 1
  let y = 2;      // this y shadows the outer one — whole fn is its TDZ
}

The inner let y shadows the outer, so the reference resolves to the inner binding, which is still in its TDZ.

typeof a;       // ❌ ReferenceError if a is a let in TDZ
typeof undeclared; // ✅ 'undefined' (never declared)

How to say it in an interview

“The Temporal Dead Zone is the gap between entering a scope and the let/const declaration line. The binding is hoisted but uninitialised, so touching it there throws a ReferenceError — that’s why let feels un-hoisted and why it catches use-before-declare bugs.”

question: Spread vs Rest operator.
answer: Although both use the ... syntax, they perform opposite operations. Spread expands an array or object into individual elements, while Rest collects multiple values into an array or object.
tip: Spread 'spreads out'; Rest 'collects the rest'.
deep:
Spread Operator (...)

The Spread operator expands an array or object into individual elements. It is commonly used for copying arrays/objects, merging them, passing arguments to functions, and inserting elements.

const a = [1, 2];
const b = [3, 4];

const merged = [...a, ...b];
// [1, 2, 3, 4]
const user = { name: 'John', age: 25 };

const updated = {
  ...user,
  city: 'London'
};

Spread creates a shallow copy. Nested objects are still shared.

Rest Operator (...)

The Rest operator collects multiple values into a single array or object. It is commonly used in function parameters and destructuring.

function sum(...numbers) {
  return numbers.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);
}

sum(10, 20, 30);
// 60
const numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40];
const [first, ...others] = numbers;

console.log(first);  // 10
console.log(others); // [20, 30, 40]
const user = {
  id: 1,
  name: 'John',
  age: 25,
  city: 'London'
};

const { id, ...details } = user;

console.log(details);
// { name: 'John', age: 25, city: 'London' }

Spread vs Rest

SpreadRest
Expands valuesCollects values
Used while passing or copying dataUsed while receiving data
Merges arrays/objectsCollects remaining arguments/elements
const arr = [...a, ...b]function fn(...args)

Common Interview Questions

  • Can Spread be used on objects? Yes, it can copy and merge objects.
  • Can Rest appear anywhere? No. Rest must always be the last parameter or destructured element.
  • Does Spread create a deep copy? No. It creates only a shallow copy.

How to say it in an interview

“Spread and Rest share the same ... syntax but perform opposite operations. Spread expands arrays or objects into individual values and is used for copying, merging, or passing arguments. Rest collects multiple values into a single array or object and is used in function parameters and destructuring. I remember it as: Spread spreads out, Rest collects the rest.”

question: null vs undefined.
answer:
undefined — a variable declared but not assigned, or a missing property/return. Set by the engine.

null — an intentional 'no value', set by the developer.

Quirk: typeof null is 'object' (a historical bug).
tip: Use ?? (nullish coalescing) to default only on null/undefined, not on 0 or ''.
code: const name = input ?? 'Guest'; // keeps 0 and '' but replaces null/undefined

question: What is a higher-order function?
answer: A function that takes a function as an argument and/or returns a function. Examples: map, filter, setTimeout, and any function factory.
tip: Debounce, throttle, and curry are all higher-order functions.
code: const withLog = fn => (...args) => { console.log(args); return fn(...args); };

question: What is currying?
answer: Currying is a functional programming technique that transforms a function with multiple arguments into a sequence of functions, each taking one argument. Each function remembers its argument using a closure until all required arguments are provided. Currying enables partial application, code reuse, and function composition.
tip: Remember: Currying = One argument at a time. It works because of JavaScript closures.
deep:
What is Currying?

Currying is a technique where a function that normally accepts multiple arguments is transformed into a sequence of functions that each accept a single argument. Every function returns another function until all required arguments have been supplied.

// Normal Function
function add(a, b, c) {
  return a + b + c;
}

add(1, 2, 3); // 6
// Curried Version
const add = a => b => c => a + b + c;

add(1)(2)(3); // 6

How does it work?

Currying relies on closures. Each returned function remembers the arguments passed to the previous function.

add(1)
   ↓ remembers a = 1
returns function
   ↓
add(1)(2)
   ↓ remembers b = 2
returns function
   ↓
add(1)(2)(3)
   ↓
returns 6

Partial Application

One of the biggest advantages of currying is creating specialized functions by fixing some arguments.

const multiply = a => b => a * b;

const double = multiply(2);
const triple = multiply(3);

console.log(double(8)); // 16
console.log(triple(8)); // 24

Instead of repeatedly writing multiplication by 2, we create a reusable double() function.

Real-world Example

Imagine an API client.

const request = baseUrl => endpoint =>
  fetch(baseUrl + endpoint);

const api = request('https://api.example.com');

api('/users');
api('/orders');

The base URL is supplied once and reused for every request.

Currying vs Normal Function

Normal FunctionCurried Function
Accepts all arguments togetherAccepts one argument at a time
Simple to callSupports partial application
Less reusableHighly reusable
Common in OOPCommon in Functional Programming

Currying vs Partial Application

These terms are often confused.

  • Currying transforms a multi-argument function into nested single-argument functions.
  • Partial Application fixes some arguments of a function, producing a new function with fewer parameters.
// Currying
const add = a => b => c => a + b + c;

// Partial Application
const add5 = add(5);
const add5And10 = add5(10);
console.log(add5And10(20)); // 35

Where is Currying Used?

  • Functional programming libraries like Lodash and Ramda.
  • Redux and NgRx selectors.
  • React Higher-Order Components.
  • Event handlers.
  • Reusable utility functions.

Interview Tip

Interviewers usually ask 'How does currying work?' Mention that it is implemented using closures. Each returned function remembers the previous argument until all arguments are collected.

Interview Answer

"Currying is the process of converting a function that takes multiple arguments into a chain of functions that each take one argument. It works using closures, allowing each function to remember previously supplied arguments. Currying makes code more reusable by enabling partial application, which is commonly used in functional programming and utility libraries."

question: What is event delegation?
answer: Event delegation is a technique where you attach a single event listener to a parent element instead of adding listeners to each child. It works because events bubble from the target element up to its parent, allowing the parent to handle events from its children.
tip: Use event delegation for lists, tables, menus, or dynamically added elements to improve performance and reduce the number of event listeners.
deep:
Why do we need Event Delegation?

Imagine a list containing hundreds of items. One approach is to attach a separate click event listener to every item.

const items = document.querySelectorAll('.item');

items.forEach(item => {
  item.addEventListener('click', () => {
    console.log(item.textContent);
  });
});

This works, but creates hundreds of event listeners, consuming more memory and becoming harder to maintain.

Using Event Delegation

Instead, attach one event listener to the parent element. When a child is clicked, the event bubbles to the parent, where it can be handled.

const list = document.getElementById('list');

list.addEventListener('click', (event) => {
  const item = event.target.closest('.item');

  if (item) {
    console.log(item.dataset.id);
  }
});

The parent receives every click because of event bubbling. The clicked element is available through event.target.

event.target vs currentTarget

  • event.target → The element that actually triggered the event.
  • event.currentTarget → The element whose event listener is currently executing.
list.addEventListener('click', (event) => {
  console.log(event.target);        // Clicked child
  console.log(event.currentTarget); // Parent (list)
});

Why use closest()?

Users may click on nested elements like an icon or a span inside a button. closest() finds the nearest matching ancestor.

<li class="item">
  <span>Delete</span>
</li>
const item = event.target.closest('.item');

This works whether the user clicks the li or the nested span.

Dynamic Elements

One of the biggest advantages of event delegation is that it automatically works for elements added later.

const li = document.createElement('li');
li.className = 'item';
li.textContent = 'New Item';

list.appendChild(li);

No new event listener is required because the parent is already listening.

Without vs With Event Delegation

Without DelegationWith Delegation
One listener per childOne listener on the parent
More memory usageLess memory usage
Need listeners for new elementsWorks automatically for new elements
Harder to maintainCleaner and simpler code

Common Use Cases

  • Lists with many items
  • Tables
  • Menus and navigation bars
  • Todo applications
  • Dynamically generated content

Common Interview Questions

  • Why is event delegation more efficient? Because it uses one event listener instead of many.
  • Why does event delegation work? Because of event bubbling.
  • Does it work for dynamically added elements? Yes, since the listener is attached to the parent.

How to say it in an interview

"Event delegation is a technique where I attach a single event listener to a parent element instead of adding listeners to every child. It works because events bubble from the child to the parent. Inside the handler, I use event.target or closest() to identify the clicked child. This improves performance, reduces memory usage, and automatically supports dynamically added elements."

question: Event bubbling vs capturing.
answer:
When an event fires, it travels capturing phase (top → target) then bubbling phase (target → top). Listeners run in the bubbling phase by default; pass { capture: true } for the capture phase.

stopPropagation() halts travel; preventDefault() cancels the default action.
tip: stopPropagation and preventDefault do different things — don't confuse them.
deep:
The three phases of event flow

Click a nested element and the event travels the DOM in order:

  1. Capturing — window → down toward the target.
  2. Target — reaches the element you interacted with.
  3. Bubbling — target → back up to window.

By default listeners fire in the bubbling phase. Pass { capture: true } (or true as the 3rd arg) to fire in the capturing phase instead.

el.addEventListener('click', fn);                 // bubbling
el.addEventListener('click', fn, { capture: true }); // capturing

Event delegation — the big payoff

Because events bubble, you can put one listener on a parent instead of many on children — great for lists and dynamically added items:

list.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
  const li = e.target.closest('li');
  if (li) select(li.dataset.id);
});

target vs currentTarget

  • e.target — the element that actually triggered the event (deepest).
  • e.currentTarget — the element whose listener is running (where you attached it).

stopPropagation vs preventDefault — not the same

MethodDoes
stopPropagation()stops the event travelling further (no more bubbling/capturing)
stopImmediatePropagation()that, plus skips other listeners on the same element
preventDefault()cancels the browser's default action (link nav, form submit) — travel continues

Non-bubbling events exist too — focus, blur, mouseenter (use focusin/mouseover if you need delegation).

How to say it in an interview

“An event captures down from the root to the target, then bubbles back up; listeners run on the bubbling phase unless I set capture: true. I lean on bubbling for event delegation — one listener on a parent. stopPropagation halts the travel, while preventDefault cancels the default action — different jobs.”

question: What is callback hell and how do you avoid it?
answer: Callback Hell is a situation where asynchronous callbacks become deeply nested, making code hard to read and maintain. It is commonly avoided using Promises and async/await.
tip: Callbacks aren't bad—deeply nesting them is. Prefer Promises or async/await for sequential async code.
deep:
What is a Callback?

A callback is simply a function passed as an argument to another function. It is executed later, usually after an asynchronous operation like an API call, timer, or file read completes.

setTimeout(() => {
  console.log('Hello');
}, 1000);

Here, the arrow function is the callback.

What is Callback Hell?

Callback Hell occurs when multiple asynchronous operations depend on one another, resulting in deeply nested callbacks. This creates the famous Pyramid of Doom, making the code difficult to read, debug, and maintain.

getUser(1, (user) => {
  getOrders(user, (orders) => {
    getPayment(orders, (payment) => {
      console.log(payment);
    });
  });
});

Why is it a Problem?

  • Code becomes deeply nested.
  • Hard to understand and maintain.
  • Error handling becomes repetitive.
  • Difficult to reuse logic.
  • Adding another async step increases nesting further.

Solution 1 — Promises

Promises flatten asynchronous code and centralize error handling.

getUser(1)
  .then(user => getOrders(user))
  .then(orders => getPayment(orders))
  .then(result => console.log(result))
  .catch(err => console.error(err));

The code now reads from top to bottom and uses a single catch() for errors.

Solution 2 — async / await (Recommended)

async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises and is the preferred modern approach.

async function processOrder() {
  try {
    const user = await getUser(1);
    const orders = await getOrders(user);
    const payment = await getPayment(orders);

    console.log(payment);
  } catch (err) {
    console.error(err);
  }
}

This looks almost identical to synchronous code while still being asynchronous.

Real-world Example

Imagine an e-commerce website.

  1. Fetch user details.
  2. Fetch user's orders.
  3. Calculate total amount.
  4. Make payment.
  5. Send confirmation email.

Using callbacks would produce deeply nested code, whereas Promises or async/await keep each step clear and readable.

Callback Hell vs Promises vs async/await

ApproachReadabilityError Handling
CallbacksLowRepeated at every level
PromisesGoodSingle catch()
async/awaitExcellentNormal try/catch

Interview Tip

Interviewers often ask whether callbacks are bad. The answer is No. Callbacks themselves are perfectly fine. The problem arises when many dependent asynchronous operations are nested inside each other, creating Callback Hell.

Interview Answer

"Callback Hell is a situation where multiple asynchronous callbacks become deeply nested, making the code difficult to read, maintain, and debug. Modern JavaScript solves this using Promises and async/await, which flatten the code structure and provide much cleaner error handling through catch() or try/catch."

question: How do you make an object immutable?
answer: You can make an object immutable using Object.freeze(). It prevents adding, removing, or modifying an object's properties. However, Object.freeze() is shallow, meaning nested objects can still be modified unless they are also frozen.
tip: Object.freeze() freezes only the top-level object. Nested objects require recursive freezing for complete immutability.
deep:
What is Immutability?

An immutable object cannot be modified after it is created. Instead of changing the existing object, you create a new one with the updated values.

Using Object.freeze()

Object.freeze() makes an object read-only by preventing:

  • Adding new properties
  • Deleting existing properties
  • Modifying existing properties
const user = Object.freeze({
  name: 'John',
  age: 25
});

user.age = 30;
user.city = 'London';
delete user.name;

console.log(user);
// { name: 'John', age: 25 }

In non-strict mode these operations are ignored. In Strict Mode, they throw an error.

Object.freeze() is Shallow

Object.freeze() only freezes the top-level object. Nested objects remain mutable.

const user = Object.freeze({
  name: 'John',
  address: {
    city: 'London'
  }
});

user.address.city = 'Paris';

console.log(user.address.city);
// Paris

Even though user is frozen, address is not.

Deep Freeze

To make an object completely immutable, every nested object must also be frozen.

function deepFreeze(obj) {
  Object.keys(obj).forEach(key => {
    if (typeof obj[key] === 'object' && obj[key] !== null) {
      deepFreeze(obj[key]);
    }
  });

  return Object.freeze(obj);
}
const user = deepFreeze({
  name: 'John',
  address: {
    city: 'London'
  }
});

Creating a New Object Instead of Modifying

Instead of changing an existing object, create a new one using the spread operator.

const user = {
  name: 'John',
  age: 25
};

const updatedUser = {
  ...user,
  age: 26
};

This approach is widely used in React, Angular (OnPush), NgRx, and Redux.

Common Interview Questions

  • Does Object.freeze() make nested objects immutable? No. It only freezes the top level.
  • How do you achieve deep immutability? Recursively freeze nested objects (deep freeze) or use an immutability library.
  • Why is immutability important? It makes code more predictable, prevents accidental changes, and helps frameworks detect updates efficiently.

How to say it in an interview

"I can make an object immutable using Object.freeze(), which prevents adding, deleting, or modifying its top-level properties. Since it is shallow, nested objects remain mutable unless they are also frozen. In modern applications, we often create new objects using the spread operator instead of mutating existing ones, especially in state management libraries like NgRx and Redux."

question: When would you use a Set or a Map?
answer: Use a Set when you need to store unique values or quickly check if a value exists. Use a Map when you need to store key/value pairs where keys can be of any data type (including objects), while preserving insertion order.
tip: Set removes duplicates. Map stores key/value pairs with any type of key.
deep:
What is a Set?

A Set is a collection of unique values. Duplicate values are automatically ignored. Sets are useful when you need uniqueness or fast existence checks.

const numbers = new Set([1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4]);

console.log(numbers);
// Set(4) {1, 2, 3, 4}

You can easily remove duplicates from an array using:

const arr = [1, 1, 2, 3, 3];
const unique = [...new Set(arr)];

console.log(unique);
// [1, 2, 3]

Useful Set methods:

const set = new Set();

set.add(10);
set.add(20);
set.has(10);      // true
set.delete(20);
set.size;
set.clear();

What is a Map?

A Map stores data as key/value pairs. Unlike plain JavaScript objects, a Map allows any data type as a key, including objects, arrays, functions, and numbers. It also preserves insertion order.

const map = new Map();

map.set('name', 'John');
map.set(1, 'One');
map.set(true, 'Yes');

console.log(map.get('name'));
// John

Objects can also be used as keys:

const user = { id: 1 };

const map = new Map();
map.set(user, 'Developer');

console.log(map.get(user));
// Developer

Useful Map methods:

const map = new Map();

map.set('age', 25);
map.get('age');
map.has('age');
map.delete('age');
map.size;
map.clear();

Object vs Map

ObjectMap
Keys are strings or symbolsKeys can be any data type
Not optimized for frequent additions/removalsOptimized for frequent insertions and deletions
Need Object.keys() to get sizeUse map.size
Primarily used for structured objectsBest for dynamic key/value collections

When should you use each?

  • Use Set when you need unique values, remove duplicates, or perform fast existence checks.
  • Use Map when you need key/value pairs with keys of any type or frequently add and remove entries.

Real-world Examples

  • Set: Store unique user IDs, unique tags, visited pages, or selected items.
  • Map: Cache API responses, store object metadata, maintain user sessions, or map DOM elements to data.

Interview Tip

A common interview question is: Why use a Map instead of an Object? The answer is that Map supports keys of any data type, preserves insertion order, provides built-in methods like set(), get(), has(), and delete(), and is better suited for dynamic key/value collections.

How to say it in an interview

"I use a Set when I need unique values or fast existence checks. I use a Map when I need key/value pairs with keys of any type, such as objects. Compared to plain objects, Maps provide cleaner APIs, preserve insertion order, and are better for frequently changing collections."

question: What do ?. and ?? do?
answer:
Optional chaining ?. short-circuits to undefined instead of throwing when accessing a property on null/undefined.

Nullish coalescing ?? returns the right side only when the left is null/undefined (unlike ||, it keeps 0, '', and false).
tip: Combine them: user?.profile?.name ?? 'Guest'.
code: const city = user?.address?.city ?? 'Unknown';

question: Function declaration vs expression vs arrow function.
answer:
• Function Declaration — fully hoisted, can be called before its definition, has its own this and arguments.
  • Function Expression — assigned to a variable, not callable before initialization, commonly used for callbacks and closures.
  • Arrow Function — concise syntax, lexically binds this, has no own arguments, prototype, or new. Best suited for callbacks and functional programming.
deep:
1. Function Declaration

A function declaration defines a named function using the function keyword. It is fully hoisted, meaning it can be invoked before its declaration because both the function name and implementation are moved to memory during the creation phase.

sayHello();

function sayHello() {
  console.log('Hello');
}

2. Function Expression

A function expression assigns a function to a variable. Only the variable is hoisted, not the function itself. If declared using const or let, it cannot be used before initialization due to the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ).

const greet = function () {
  console.log('Hello');
};

greet();
greet(); // ❌ ReferenceError
const greet = function() {};

3. Arrow Function

Arrow functions provide a shorter syntax and inherit this from their surrounding scope. They do not create their own this, arguments, or prototype, and therefore cannot be used as constructors.

const add = (a, b) => a + b;

Lexical this

The biggest difference is how this behaves.

const user = {
  name: 'John',

  normal() {
    console.log(this.name);
  },

  arrow: () => {
    console.log(this.name);
  }
};

user.normal(); // John
user.arrow();  // undefined (or window.name)

The normal function gets this from the object calling it. The arrow function inherits this from the outer scope.

Can Arrow Functions be Constructors?

No. Arrow functions do not have a prototype, so they cannot be called with new.

const Person = (name) => {
  this.name = name;
};

new Person('John'); // ❌ TypeError

Comparison

FeatureDeclarationExpressionArrow
Hoisted✅ Yes❌ No❌ No
Own this✅ Yes✅ Yes❌ Lexical
Own arguments✅ Yes✅ Yes❌ No
Constructor✅ Yes✅ Yes❌ No
prototype✅ Yes✅ Yes❌ No

When should you use each?

  • Function Declaration – Utility functions and reusable business logic.
  • Function Expression – Callbacks, closures, factory functions, event handlers.
  • Arrow Function – Array methods (map, filter, reduce), Promise callbacks, RxJS operators, and places where lexical this is desired.

Interview Tip

Interviewers almost always ask about this. Mention that arrow functions inherit this from the surrounding scope, making them ideal for callbacks but a poor choice for object methods or constructors.

Interview Answer

"Function declarations are fully hoisted and can be called before they are defined. Function expressions are assigned to variables and cannot be used before initialization. Arrow functions provide concise syntax and lexically bind this, but they don't have their own arguments or prototype, so they cannot be used as constructors. I typically use declarations for reusable functions, expressions for callbacks, and arrows for array methods, Promises, and RxJS."
tip: Never use an arrow as an object method that needs `this`, or as a constructor.
code:
function decl() {}         // hoisted
const expr = function () {};// not hoisted
const arrow = () => {};     // lexical this

question: What is an IIFE and the module pattern?
answer: An IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression) is a function that runs immediately after it is created. It creates its own private scope, so variables inside it cannot be accessed from outside. The Module Pattern uses an IIFE to keep data private while exposing only the functions that should be accessible.
tip: Before ES6 modules, IIFEs were commonly used to avoid polluting the global scope and to create private variables.
deep:
What is an IIFE?

IIFE stands for Immediately Invoked Function Expression. It is a function that executes immediately after it is defined.

(function () {
  console.log('Hello');
})();

The function runs immediately and is never called again unless another IIFE is created.

Why use an IIFE?

An IIFE creates a private scope. Variables declared inside it cannot be accessed from outside, preventing accidental modification and avoiding global variables.

(function () {
  const message = 'Hello';
})();

console.log(message);
// ReferenceError

What is the Module Pattern?

The Module Pattern uses an IIFE together with closures to keep some variables private while exposing only selected methods.

const counter = (function () {
  let count = 0;

  return {
    increment() {
      count++;
    },
    getCount() {
      return count;
    }
  };
})();

Here, count is private. It cannot be accessed directly.

console.log(counter.count);
// undefined

The only way to modify it is through the public methods.

counter.increment();
console.log(counter.getCount());
// 1

How does it work?

  1. The IIFE executes immediately.
  2. It creates private variables.
  3. It returns an object containing public methods.
  4. The returned methods continue to access the private variables through closures.

Why was it popular?

Before ES6 Modules (import/export), JavaScript had no built-in module system. Developers used the Module Pattern to organize code, hide implementation details, and avoid global variables.

IIFE vs ES Modules

IIFE / Module PatternES Modules
Older approachModern standard
Uses closures for privacyUses import and export
Immediately executesLoaded when imported
Avoids global variablesProvides built-in module support

Common Interview Questions

  • What does IIFE stand for? Immediately Invoked Function Expression.
  • Why use an IIFE? To create a private scope and avoid global variables.
  • How does the Module Pattern provide encapsulation? By keeping variables private inside an IIFE and exposing only selected methods.
  • Is the Module Pattern still used today? It is mostly replaced by ES Modules, but you'll still see it in legacy JavaScript code.

How to say it in an interview

"An IIFE is a function that executes immediately after it is defined. It creates a private scope, preventing variables from leaking into the global scope. The Module Pattern builds on this idea by returning only the methods that should be public while keeping internal data private using closures. Today, ES Modules have largely replaced this pattern, but it's still common in older JavaScript codebases."

question: Explain destructuring with defaults and renaming.
answer: Unpack arrays/objects into variables in one statement, with defaults for missing values and renaming for objects. Also enables clean swaps and nested extraction.
tip: Destructuring function params with defaults gives clean, self-documenting APIs.

question: Object.keys(), Object.values(), Object.entries() and Object.fromEntries().
answer: These methods help you work with JavaScript objects. Object.keys() returns an array of property names, Object.values() returns an array of values, Object.entries() returns key-value pairs, and Object.fromEntries() converts key-value pairs back into an object.
tip: A common interview pattern is Object.entries() → map() → Object.fromEntries() to transform an object's values.
deep:
Why are these methods useful?

Objects are not directly iterable like arrays. These methods convert an object into arrays so you can use array methods such as map(), filter(), and reduce().

Object.keys()

Returns an array containing all property names (keys).

const user = {
  name: 'John',
  age: 25
};

console.log(Object.keys(user));
// ['name', 'age']

Object.values()

Returns an array containing all property values.

console.log(Object.values(user));
// ['John', 25]

Object.entries()

Returns an array of [key, value] pairs.

console.log(Object.entries(user));
// [
//   ['name', 'John'],
//   ['age', 25]
// ]

This is useful when you need both the key and value.

Object.fromEntries()

Converts an array of key-value pairs back into an object.

const entries = [
  ['name', 'John'],
  ['age', 25]
];

const obj = Object.fromEntries(entries);

console.log(obj);
// { name: 'John', age: 25 }

Transforming an Object

A very common pattern is to convert an object into entries, modify the entries, and then convert them back into an object.

const prices = {
  apple: 10,
  banana: 20
};

const doubled = Object.fromEntries(
  Object.entries(prices).map(([key, value]) => [key, value * 2])
);

console.log(doubled);
// { apple: 20, banana: 40 }

Comparison

MethodReturns
Object.keys()Array of property names
Object.values()Array of property values
Object.entries()Array of [key, value] pairs
Object.fromEntries()Creates an object from key-value pairs

Common Interview Questions

  • Can you use map() directly on an object? No. Convert it using Object.entries() first.
  • Which method returns both keys and values? Object.entries().
  • Which method converts an array back into an object? Object.fromEntries().

How to say it in an interview

"These methods help us work with objects more easily. Object.keys() returns property names, Object.values() returns property values, Object.entries() returns key-value pairs, and Object.fromEntries() converts those pairs back into an object. A common pattern is Object.entries() → map() → Object.fromEntries() to transform an object."

question: JSON.stringify / parse — gotchas?
answer:
JSON.stringify() converts a JavaScript object into a JSON string, while JSON.parse() converts a JSON string back into a JavaScript object.

Common gotchas: undefined, functions, and symbols are omitted; Date objects become ISO strings; NaN and Infinity become null; BigInt throws an error; circular references cause JSON.stringify() to fail. Use a replacer and reviver to customize serialization and deserialization.
tip: JSON.stringify(obj, null, 2) pretty-prints — handy for debugging and exports.
deep:
What is JSON?

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight text format used to exchange data between applications. APIs usually send and receive JSON strings.

JSON.stringify()

JSON.stringify() converts a JavaScript object into a JSON string.

const user = {
  name: 'John',
  age: 25
};

const json = JSON.stringify(user);
console.log(json);
// {"name":"John","age":25}

JSON.parse()

JSON.parse() converts a JSON string back into a JavaScript object.

const json = '{"name":"John","age":25}';
const user = JSON.parse(json);
console.log(user.name);

Common Gotchas

ValueResult after JSON.stringify()
undefinedRemoved
FunctionRemoved
SymbolRemoved
DateConverted to ISO String
NaNnull
Infinitynull
BigIntThrows TypeError
JSON.stringify({
  value: undefined,
  fn() {},
  date: new Date(),
  score: NaN
});

Circular Reference

Objects that reference themselves cannot be serialized.

const obj = {};
obj.self = obj;

JSON.stringify(obj);
// TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON

Replacer Function

The replacer lets you modify values while converting an object into JSON.

const json = JSON.stringify(user, (key, value) => {
  if (key === 'password') return undefined;
  return value;
});

Reviver Function

The reviver transforms values while parsing JSON.

const user = JSON.parse(json, (key, value) => {
  if (key === 'createdAt') {
    return new Date(value);
  }
  return value;
});

JSON is NOT a Deep Clone Solution

Many developers use:

const copy = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj));

Although this creates a deep copy for simple objects, it loses important information such as Dates, Maps, Sets, functions, undefined values, and class instances. In modern JavaScript, use structuredClone() when available.

const clone = structuredClone(obj);

Real-world Example

Suppose an Angular application receives customer data from a Spring Boot backend.

Spring Boot
      ↓
JSON Response
      ↓
HttpClient
      ↓
JSON.parse()
      ↓
JavaScript Object
      ↓
Angular UI

Similarly, when submitting a form, Angular converts the object into JSON before sending it to the backend.

Interview Tip

Interviewers often ask whether JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) is a good way to deep clone an object. Explain that it only works for simple JSON-compatible objects and loses Dates, Maps, Sets, functions, undefined values, and class instances. Prefer structuredClone() for modern applications.

Interview Answer

"JSON.stringify() converts a JavaScript object into a JSON string, while JSON.parse() converts a JSON string back into an object. Common pitfalls include losing undefined values, functions, Symbols, Dates becoming strings, NaN and Infinity becoming null, BigInt throwing an error, and circular references failing. Replacer and reviver functions allow custom serialization and deserialization, and structuredClone() is preferred over the JSON trick for deep cloning modern JavaScript objects."

question: How does fetch() work, and how do you cancel a request?
answer: fetch() is used to make HTTP requests. It returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object. Unlike many developers expect, fetch() does not throw an error for HTTP status codes like 404 or 500; you must check response.ok yourself. To cancel an ongoing request, use an AbortController.
tip: Always check response.ok before reading the response. Use AbortController to cancel requests when a component is destroyed or a user navigates away.
deep:
What is fetch()?

fetch() is the modern JavaScript API used to send HTTP requests such as GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE. It returns a Promise, making it easy to work with asynchronous operations.

fetch('https://api.example.com/users')
  .then(response => response.json())
  .then(data => console.log(data));

How does fetch() work?

  1. Send an HTTP request.
  2. Receive a Response object.
  3. Check whether the request succeeded using response.ok.
  4. Convert the response into JSON, text, blob, etc.
  5. Process the returned data.

Checking for HTTP Errors

A common misconception is that fetch() throws an error for HTTP errors such as 404 or 500. It does not.

fetch('/users')
  .then(response => {
    if (!response.ok) {
      throw new Error('HTTP Error: ' + response.status);
    }
    return response.json();
  });

The Promise is rejected only for network errors, such as no internet connection or a request being aborted.

Using async/await

async function loadUsers() {
  const response = await fetch('/users');

  if (!response.ok) {
    throw new Error('Request failed');
  }

  const users = await response.json();
  console.log(users);
}

Cancelling a Request

You can cancel a request using AbortController.

const controller = new AbortController();

fetch('/users', {
  signal: controller.signal
});

controller.abort();

When the request is aborted, the Promise is rejected with an AbortError.

Why cancel a request?

  • User navigates to another page.
  • User searches again before the previous request finishes.
  • Component is destroyed.
  • Prevent unnecessary network usage.

Common Response Methods

MethodDescription
response.json()Read JSON data
response.text()Read text data
response.blob()Read binary data
response.arrayBuffer()Read raw binary buffer

Common Interview Questions

  • Does fetch() throw an error for 404 or 500? No. Check response.ok manually.
  • When does fetch() reject? On network failures or when the request is aborted.
  • How do you cancel a fetch request? Using AbortController and its signal.
  • What does fetch() return? A Promise that resolves to a Response object.

How to say it in an interview

"fetch() is the modern API for making HTTP requests. It returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object. Unlike Axios, fetch() does not throw errors for HTTP status codes like 404 or 500, so I always check response.ok. To cancel an ongoing request, I use an AbortController, which is useful when a user navigates away or a component is destroyed."

question: Promise.all() vs Promise.allSettled() vs Promise.race() vs Promise.any().
answer: These Promise methods are used to handle multiple asynchronous operations. Promise.all() waits for all promises to succeed, Promise.allSettled() waits for all promises regardless of success or failure, Promise.race() returns the first promise that settles, and Promise.any() returns the first promise that successfully resolves.
tip: Remember: all = all success, allSettled = wait for everything, race = first finished, any = first successful.
deep:
Why do we need Promise combinators?

Sometimes multiple asynchronous operations need to run together. JavaScript provides different Promise methods depending on whether you need all results, the first result, or the first successful result.

Promise.all()

Waits until all promises are fulfilled. If any promise rejects, the entire operation immediately rejects.

Promise.all([
  fetch('/users'),
  fetch('/posts')
])
.then(results => console.log(results))
.catch(error => console.log(error));

Use when: Every request is required to succeed.

Promise.allSettled()

Waits for every promise to finish, whether it succeeds or fails.

Promise.allSettled([
  p1,
  p2,
  p3
]);

Returns an array like:

[
  { status: 'fulfilled', value: 'A' },
  { status: 'rejected', reason: 'Failed' },
  { status: 'fulfilled', value: 'C' }
]

Use when: You want every result, even if some operations fail.

Promise.race()

Returns the result of the first promise that settles, whether it resolves or rejects.

Promise.race([
  fetch('/api'),
  timeoutPromise
]);

Use when: Implementing request timeouts or taking the fastest response.

Promise.any()

Returns the first fulfilled promise. It ignores rejected promises unless they all fail.

Promise.any([
  server1(),
  server2(),
  server3()
]);

If every promise rejects, it throws an AggregateError.

Comparison

MethodWaits ForRejects WhenUse Case
Promise.all()All promises succeedFirst rejectionAll requests are required
Promise.allSettled()All promises finishNeverNeed every result
Promise.race()First settled promiseIf the first settled promise rejectsTimeouts, fastest response
Promise.any()First fulfilled promiseOnly if all promises rejectMultiple backup APIs

Common Interview Questions

  • Which method fails immediately if one promise fails? Promise.all().
  • Which method never rejects? Promise.allSettled().
  • What's the difference between race() and any()? race() returns the first settled promise (success or failure), while any() returns the first successful promise.
  • What happens if every promise rejects in Promise.any()? It throws an AggregateError.

How to say it in an interview

"I use Promise.all() when every async operation must succeed. If I need all results regardless of failures, I use Promise.allSettled(). Promise.race() gives the first promise that finishes, making it useful for timeouts, while Promise.any() returns the first successful result and ignores failures until all promises fail."

question: What are generators and iterators?
answer: An iterator is an object that allows sequential access to a collection using the next() method. A generator is a special function declared with function* that automatically creates an iterator. It can pause execution using yield and resume later, making it ideal for lazy evaluation, large datasets, and infinite sequences.
tip: Remember: Generator = Function that creates an Iterator. yield pauses execution, and next() resumes it.
deep:
What is an Iterator?

An iterator is an object that provides sequential access to values. It exposes a next() method that returns an object containing:

  • value – the current value.
  • done – whether iteration has finished.
const arr = [10, 20, 30];
const iterator = arr[Symbol.iterator]();

console.log(iterator.next());
// { value: 10, done: false }

console.log(iterator.next());
// { value: 20, done: false }

Arrays, Strings, Maps, Sets, and many built-in objects are iterable because they implement Symbol.iterator.

What is a Generator?

A generator is a special function declared using function*. Instead of returning once, it can pause execution with yield and continue from the same point when next() is called.

function* numbers() {
  yield 1;
  yield 2;
  yield 3;
}

const gen = numbers();

How yield Works

function* demo() {
  console.log('Start');
  yield 1;

  console.log('Middle');
  yield 2;

  console.log('End');
  yield 3;
}
const g = demo();

g.next();
// Start
// { value: 1, done: false }

g.next();
// Middle
// { value: 2, done: false }

g.next();
// End
// { value: 3, done: false }

Each call to next() resumes execution from the previous yield.

Iterator vs Generator

IteratorGenerator
ObjectSpecial Function
Must implement next()Automatically creates an iterator
Manual implementationEasy to write using yield
Controls iterationProduces iterable values

Infinite Sequence

Generators are useful for generating values on demand instead of storing everything in memory.

function* ids() {
  let id = 1;

  while (true) {
    yield id++;
  }
}

const gen = ids();

console.log(gen.next().value); // 1
console.log(gen.next().value); // 2
console.log(gen.next().value); // 3

The generator produces values only when requested, making it memory efficient.

Using for...of

Generators implement the iterator protocol automatically, so they work directly with for...of.

function* colors() {
  yield 'Red';
  yield 'Green';
  yield 'Blue';
}

for (const color of colors()) {
  console.log(color);
}

Real-world Uses

  • Lazy loading large datasets.
  • Infinite sequences (IDs, pagination).
  • Tree and graph traversal.
  • Custom iterators.
  • Streaming data processing.

Iterator Protocol

Any object that implements Symbol.iterator is iterable.

const person = {
  names: ['John', 'Alice'],

  *[Symbol.iterator]() {
    for (const name of this.names) {
      yield name;
    }
  }
};

for (const name of person) {
  console.log(name);
}

Interview Tip

Many interviewers ask whether generators return arrays. The answer is No. A generator returns an iterator. Values are produced one at a time only when next() is called, which makes generators memory efficient for large or infinite collections.

Interview Answer

"An iterator is an object with a next() method that returns values sequentially. A generator is a special function declared with function* that automatically creates an iterator. It uses yield to pause execution and next() to resume it. Generators are useful for lazy evaluation, custom iteration, infinite sequences, and processing large datasets efficiently because values are generated only when needed."

question: What is a Symbol and where is it used?
answer: A Symbol is a unique and immutable primitive value introduced in ES6. It is mainly used as a unique object property key so that it does not conflict with other property names. Even if two Symbols have the same description, they are always different.
tip: Use Symbols when you need unique object keys or want to customize JavaScript behavior using well-known symbols like Symbol.iterator.
deep:
What is a Symbol?

A Symbol is a primitive data type introduced in ES6. Every Symbol is unique, even if it has the same description.

const s1 = Symbol('id');
const s2 = Symbol('id');

console.log(s1 === s2);
// false

The string 'id' is only a description for debugging. It does not make Symbols equal.

Why use Symbols?

Symbols are mainly used as object property keys to avoid naming conflicts.

const id = Symbol('id');

const user = {
  name: 'John',
  [id]: 101
};

console.log(user.name);
// John

console.log(user[id]);
// 101

Since the Symbol key is unique, no other property can accidentally overwrite it.

Symbols are not included in normal iteration

Properties whose keys are Symbols are ignored by methods such as for...in, Object.keys(), and Object.values().

const id = Symbol('id');

const user = {
  name: 'John',
  [id]: 101
};

console.log(Object.keys(user));
// ['name']

To access Symbol properties, use:

Object.getOwnPropertySymbols(user);

Global Symbols

Symbol.for() creates or retrieves a Symbol from the global Symbol registry.

const s1 = Symbol.for('user');
const s2 = Symbol.for('user');

console.log(s1 === s2);
// true

Unlike Symbol(), the same key returns the same Symbol.

Well-known Symbols

JavaScript provides built-in Symbols that customize language behavior.

const numbers = {
  *[Symbol.iterator]() {
    yield 1;
    yield 2;
    yield 3;
  }
};

for (const n of numbers) {
  console.log(n);
}

Here, Symbol.iterator makes the object iterable.

Common Use Cases

  • Create unique object property names.
  • Avoid property name collisions in libraries.
  • Implement JavaScript protocols using well-known Symbols.

Common Interview Questions

  • Why use Symbol instead of a string? Because every Symbol is unique.
  • Can two Symbols with the same description be equal? No.
  • What is Symbol.for()? It returns a shared Symbol from the global registry.
  • Name a well-known Symbol. Symbol.iterator.

How to say it in an interview

"A Symbol is a unique primitive value introduced in ES6. It is commonly used as a unique object property key to avoid naming conflicts. JavaScript also provides well-known Symbols such as Symbol.iterator that allow objects to customize built-in language behavior."

question: What are WeakMap and WeakSet? When should you use them?
answer: WeakMap and WeakSet are similar to Map and Set, but they store objects only using weak references. If an object is no longer used anywhere else in the application, JavaScript can automatically remove it from a WeakMap or WeakSet during garbage collection, helping prevent memory leaks.
tip: Use WeakMap to associate extra data with objects without preventing them from being garbage collected.
deep:
What is a WeakMap?

A WeakMap stores key-value pairs like a normal Map, but its keys must always be objects.

const cache = new WeakMap();

const user = {};

cache.set(user, 'User Data');

console.log(cache.get(user));
// User Data

Why is it called 'Weak'?

The object key is stored using a weak reference. If nothing else in your program references that object, JavaScript's Garbage Collector (GC) is free to remove both the object and its WeakMap entry automatically.

This helps avoid memory leaks.

What is a WeakSet?

A WeakSet is similar to a Set, but it can only store objects.

const visited = new WeakSet();

const node = {};

visited.add(node);

console.log(visited.has(node));
// true

Limitations

  • Keys (WeakMap) and values (WeakSet) must be objects.
  • They are not iterable.
  • They do not have size.
  • You cannot loop through them using for...of.

WeakMap vs Map

MapWeakMap
Any value can be a keyOnly objects can be keys
IterableNot iterable
Has sizeNo size
Keeps objects aliveAllows garbage collection

WeakSet vs Set

SetWeakSet
Stores any valueStores only objects
IterableNot iterable
Has sizeNo size

Common Use Cases

  • Storing metadata for DOM elements.
  • Caching computed values for objects.
  • Tracking visited objects.
  • Avoiding memory leaks.

Common Interview Questions

  • Why use WeakMap instead of Map? To avoid memory leaks by allowing object keys to be garbage collected.
  • Can WeakMap keys be strings? No. Only objects.
  • Can you iterate over a WeakMap? No.
  • Does WeakMap have a size property? No.

How to say it in an interview

"WeakMap and WeakSet are similar to Map and Set, but they store objects using weak references. If an object is no longer referenced elsewhere, JavaScript can automatically remove it during garbage collection. They're mainly used for object metadata, caches, and preventing memory leaks."

question: Why does a for-loop with var log the wrong value in setTimeout()?
answer: var is function-scoped, so all setTimeout() callbacks share the same i variable. By the time they execute, the loop has finished and i has its final value. let is block-scoped, so each loop iteration gets its own copy of i, producing the expected output.
tip: Whenever you see setTimeout() inside a loop, think about var vs let and closures.
deep:
The Problem

This is one of the most common JavaScript interview questions.

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => {
    console.log(i);
  }, 1000);
}

Many developers expect the output to be:

0
1
2

But the actual output is:

3
3
3

Why does this happen?

var is function-scoped, so the entire loop shares a single i variable.

The setTimeout() callbacks don't execute immediately. They run only after the loop has completed.

By that time, i has already become 3, so every callback prints the same value.

How does let fix it?

let is block-scoped. JavaScript creates a new i for every loop iteration.

for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => {
    console.log(i);
  }, 1000);
}

Each callback closes over its own value of i.

Output:
0
1
2

How Closures are Involved

The callback passed to setTimeout() forms a closure. It remembers the variables from the surrounding scope.

  • With var, every closure references the same variable.
  • With let, every closure references a different variable created for that iteration.

Another Solution (Using an IIFE)

Before let existed, developers used an IIFE to create a new scope.

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  (function(index) {
    setTimeout(() => {
      console.log(index);
    }, 1000);
  })(i);
}
Output:
0
1
2

Comparison

Using varUsing let
Function-scopedBlock-scoped
One shared variableNew variable per iteration
Output: 3 3 3Output: 0 1 2
Common interview trapRecommended approach

Common Interview Questions

  • Why does var print 3 3 3? Because all callbacks share the same variable.
  • Why does let print 0 1 2? Because each iteration gets its own block-scoped variable.
  • Can you solve this without let? Yes, using an IIFE to create a new scope for each iteration.

How to say it in an interview

"The callback inside setTimeout() forms a closure. When using var, every callback shares the same function-scoped variable, so after the loop finishes they all print the final value. Using let creates a new block-scoped variable for each iteration, so each callback remembers its own value."

question: typeof vs instanceof.
answer: Use typeof to check the type of primitive values like strings, numbers, booleans, undefined, and functions. Use instanceof to check whether an object was created from a particular class or constructor, such as Array, Date, or custom classes.
tip: Remember: typeof is mainly for primitives, while instanceof is for objects. Use Array.isArray() to detect arrays.
code:
typeof 'Hello';          // 'string'
typeof 123;              // 'number'
typeof true;             // 'boolean'
typeof undefined;        // 'undefined'
typeof function() {};    // 'function'

typeof [];               // 'object'
Array.isArray([]);       // true
[] instanceof Array;     // true
new Date() instanceof Date; // true
deep:
typeof

typeof returns the type of a value as a string. It is mainly used for checking primitive values.

typeof 'Hello';      // 'string'
typeof 10;           // 'number'
typeof true;         // 'boolean'
typeof undefined;    // 'undefined'
typeof Symbol();     // 'symbol'
typeof 10n;          // 'bigint'
typeof function(){}; // 'function'

One confusing behavior is that arrays and null both return 'object'.

typeof [];
// 'object'

typeof null;
// 'object' (this is a well-known JavaScript bug kept for backward compatibility)

To check if a value is an array, always use Array.isArray().

Array.isArray([]);
// true

instanceof

instanceof checks whether an object was created using a particular constructor or class.

const arr = [];

console.log(arr instanceof Array);
// true

console.log(new Date() instanceof Date);
// true

It also works with custom classes.

class Person {}

const p = new Person();

console.log(p instanceof Person);
// true

typeof vs instanceof

typeofinstanceof
Checks the type of a valueChecks whether an object belongs to a class/constructor
Mainly used for primitive valuesUsed for objects
Returns a stringReturns true or false
typeof 'abc' → 'string'[] instanceof Array → true

Common Interview Questions

  • Why does typeof null return 'object'? It is a historical bug in JavaScript that remains for backward compatibility.
  • Can typeof detect arrays? No. Arrays return 'object'. Use Array.isArray().
  • Can instanceof be used with primitives? No. It is intended for objects created from constructors or classes.

How to say it in an interview

"I use typeof to check primitive data types like string, number, boolean, undefined, and function. I use instanceof to check whether an object was created from a specific constructor or class, such as Array, Date, or a custom class. For arrays, I prefer Array.isArray() because typeof [] returns 'object'."

question: How do you check for NaN correctly?
answer: Use Number.isNaN() to check if a value is NaN. Avoid the global isNaN() because it converts (coerces) values before checking, which can produce unexpected results.
tip: Use Number.isNaN() instead of isNaN() to avoid type coercion.
code:
Number.isNaN(NaN);       // true
Number.isNaN('foo');     // false

isNaN('foo');            // true (coerces 'foo' to NaN)
isNaN('123');            // false
deep:
What is NaN?

NaN stands for Not a Number. It represents the result of an invalid mathematical operation.

0 / 0;          // NaN
Math.sqrt(-1); // NaN
Number('abc'); // NaN

Why can't you compare NaN?

NaN is the only JavaScript value that is not equal to itself.

NaN === NaN;
// false

NaN == NaN;
// false

Therefore, comparing a value directly with NaN never works.

Using Number.isNaN() (Recommended)

Number.isNaN() checks whether a value is actually the NaN value. It does not perform type coercion.

Number.isNaN(NaN);      // true
Number.isNaN('abc');    // false
Number.isNaN(123);      // false
Number.isNaN(undefined);// false

Using isNaN()

The global isNaN() first converts the value to a number before checking it. This can produce confusing results.

isNaN('abc');   // true
isNaN('123');   // false
isNaN(true);    // false
isNaN(null);    // false

For example, isNaN('abc') returns true because JavaScript first converts 'abc' to NaN.

Number.isNaN() vs isNaN()

Number.isNaN()isNaN()
No type coercionPerforms type coercion
Checks only actual NaN valuesChecks whether a value becomes NaN after conversion
RecommendedAvoid unless coercion is desired

Common Interview Questions

  • Why does NaN === NaN return false? Because NaN is defined as a unique value that is never equal to itself.
  • Which method should you use? Always prefer Number.isNaN() because it performs a strict check without converting the value.

How to say it in an interview

"I use Number.isNaN() to check for NaN because it performs a strict check without type coercion. The global isNaN() converts values before checking, which can lead to unexpected results. Also, NaN === NaN is false because NaN is the only JavaScript value that is not equal to itself."

question: How do you update the DOM efficiently?
answer: To update the DOM efficiently, reduce the number of DOM changes. Instead of updating elements one by one, batch your changes and apply them together. You can use a DocumentFragment, toggle CSS classes instead of repeatedly changing inline styles, and use requestAnimationFrame() for smooth visual updates.
tip: Make multiple DOM changes together instead of updating the page repeatedly. Fewer DOM updates usually mean better performance.
deep:
Why is DOM manipulation expensive?

Updating the DOM is slower than working with normal JavaScript objects because the browser may need to recalculate the page layout and repaint the screen.

1. Batch DOM Updates

Instead of adding elements one by one, create them first and add them to the page together.

const fragment = document.createDocumentFragment();

items.forEach(item => {
  const li = document.createElement('li');
  li.textContent = item;
  fragment.appendChild(li);
});

list.appendChild(fragment);
