This is more efficient than calling appendChild() for every item directly on the page.

2. Use DocumentFragment

A DocumentFragment is a lightweight container that exists only in memory. Changes made to it do not affect the page until it is appended to the DOM.

3. Avoid Layout Thrashing

Reading layout properties like offsetWidth immediately after changing the DOM forces the browser to recalculate the layout repeatedly.

element.style.width = '200px';
console.log(element.offsetWidth); // Forces layout calculation

Try to group reads together and writes together.

4. Toggle CSS Classes

Instead of changing multiple inline styles, add or remove a CSS class.

element.classList.add('active');
element.classList.remove('active');

This keeps code cleaner and allows the browser to optimize rendering.

5. Use requestAnimationFrame()

For animations or frequent UI updates, use requestAnimationFrame(). It schedules updates just before the browser repaints the screen, making animations smoother.

requestAnimationFrame(() => {
  box.style.left = '100px';
});

Best Practices

  • Batch multiple DOM updates.
  • Use DocumentFragment when creating many elements.
  • Avoid unnecessary layout calculations.
  • Prefer CSS classes over repeated inline style changes.
  • Use requestAnimationFrame() for animations.

Common Interview Questions

  • Why is DOM manipulation slow? Because it can trigger layout recalculation and repainting.
  • What is a DocumentFragment? A lightweight container used to build DOM elements before inserting them into the document.
  • Why use requestAnimationFrame()? It synchronizes visual updates with the browser's rendering cycle.

How to say it in an interview

"To improve DOM performance, I minimize the number of DOM updates by batching changes. I use DocumentFragment when creating many elements, avoid unnecessary layout recalculations, prefer toggling CSS classes instead of repeatedly changing inline styles, and use requestAnimationFrame() for smooth animations."

question: What is a pure function and why prefer it?
answer: A pure function always produces the same output for the same input and does not cause side effects. It does not modify external variables, mutate its arguments, perform I/O operations, or depend on external state. Pure functions are predictable, easy to test, reusable, cacheable (memoization), and form the foundation of functional programming, Redux, and NgRx reducers.
tip: Reducers in Redux/NgRx must be pure — that's what enables time-travel debugging.
deep:
What is a Pure Function?

A pure function satisfies two important rules:

  1. It always returns the same output for the same input.
  2. It has no side effects.

This means the function depends only on its parameters and does not modify or rely on anything outside itself.

Pure Function Example

function add(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

add(2, 3); // 5
add(2, 3); // 5

No matter how many times it is called with the same arguments, the result is always identical.

Impure Function Example

let total = 0;

function addToTotal(value) {
  total += value;
  return total;
}

This function is impure because it modifies an external variable. Calling addToTotal(5) twice returns different values.

addToTotal(5); // 5
addToTotal(5); // 10

What are Side Effects?

A side effect is any operation that changes something outside the function or depends on external state.

Examples include:

  • Modifying global variables.
  • Changing function arguments.
  • Updating the DOM.
  • Making API calls.
  • Reading or writing files.
  • Writing to localStorage.
  • Logging to the console (technically a side effect).

Mutating Parameters

Changing the input object also makes a function impure.

// ❌ Impure
function rename(user) {
  user.name = 'John';
}

// ✅ Pure
function rename(user) {
  return {
    ...user,
    name: 'John'
  };
}

Why Prefer Pure Functions?

BenefitReason
PredictableSame input always produces the same output.
Easy to TestNo mocks or setup for external state.
ReusableNo dependency on surrounding code.
MemoizationResults can be safely cached.
DebuggingBehavior is deterministic.
Parallel ExecutionNo shared mutable state.

Real-world Example

NgRx reducers are required to be pure functions.

export const counterReducer = createReducer(
  initialState,
  on(increment, state => ({
    ...state,
    count: state.count + 1
  }))
);

The reducer never modifies the existing state. Instead, it returns a new state object, making change detection and time-travel debugging possible.

Pure Functions and Angular

  • NgRx Reducers must be pure.
  • Pure Pipes execute only when input values change.
  • OnPush Change Detection works best with immutable data and pure functions.

Interview Tip

Interviewers often ask whether a function that calls an API or updates the DOM is pure. The answer is No, because those operations produce side effects. Mention that reducers and pure pipes are common real-world examples of pure functions.

Interview Answer

"A pure function always returns the same output for the same input and has no side effects. It doesn't modify external variables, mutate its arguments, or depend on external state. Pure functions are easier to test, debug, cache, and reason about, which is why frameworks like Redux and NgRx require reducers to be pure."

question: What are the data types in JavaScript?
answer:
Primitive (immutable, copied by value): string, number, boolean, bigint, symbol, undefined, null.

Non-primitive (reference type): object — which includes arrays, functions, and dates.
tip: Primitives are compared/copied by value; objects by reference — a very common gotcha.
code:
typeof 'a';     // 'string'
typeof 10n;     // 'bigint'
typeof null;    // 'object' (historical bug)
typeof [];      // 'object'

question: How do you convert between strings and numbers?
answer:
Number → string: String(n), n.toString(), or a template literal `${n}`.

String → number: Number(s), parseInt(s) (integer), parseFloat(s) (decimal), or the unary +s.
tip: parseInt('123px') = 123 (stops at non-digits); Number('123px') = NaN (strict).

question: Synchronous vs asynchronous programming.
answer:
Synchronous programming executes code one statement at a time. Each operation must finish before the next one starts, so long-running tasks block the execution.

Asynchronous programming allows time-consuming operations like API calls, timers, and file I/O to run without blocking the main thread. Once completed, the result is handled later using callbacks, Promises, or async/await, keeping the application responsive.
tip: JS is single-threaded, so async is how it stays responsive during I/O.
deep:
What is Synchronous Programming?

In synchronous programming, JavaScript executes one statement at a time. Every line waits for the previous one to finish before executing. If one operation takes a long time, the entire program waits.

console.log('Start');

function longTask() {
  for (let i = 0; i < 1e9; i++) {}
}

longTask();
console.log('End');

The UI becomes unresponsive until longTask() finishes because JavaScript executes everything on a single main thread.

What is Asynchronous Programming?

Asynchronous programming allows JavaScript to start a long-running operation without blocking the main thread. The operation runs outside the JavaScript engine (through browser APIs or Node.js APIs), and once completed, its callback is queued for execution.

console.log('Start');

setTimeout(() => {
  console.log('Timer Finished');
}, 2000);

console.log('End');

Output:

Start
End
Timer Finished

JavaScript does not wait for the timer. Instead, it continues executing the remaining synchronous code.

How Async Works

Call Stack
     ↓
Web APIs (setTimeout, fetch, DOM Events)
     ↓
Callback Queue / Microtask Queue
     ↓
Event Loop
     ↓
Call Stack

The Event Loop continuously checks whether the Call Stack is empty. If it is, it moves ready callbacks into the stack for execution.

Real-world Example

Imagine an Angular application fetching customers from a Spring Boot backend.

Component
     ↓
HttpClient.get()
     ↓
Network Request
     ↓
Backend API
     ↓
Response
     ↓
Observable emits data
     ↓
UI Updates

While the request is in progress, the user can continue interacting with the application because the network request is asynchronous.

Synchronous vs Asynchronous

SynchronousAsynchronous
Runs line by lineDoes not block the main thread
BlockingNon-blocking
Simple flowUses callbacks, Promises, or async/await
Poor for long-running tasksIdeal for API calls, timers, and file operations

Ways to Write Async Code

  • Callbacks (older approach)
  • Promises (better error handling)
  • async/await (modern and most readable)
// Promise
fetch('/users')
  .then(res => res.json())
  .then(console.log);

// async/await
async function loadUsers() {
  const res = await fetch('/users');
  const users = await res.json();
  console.log(users);
}

Common Misconception

JavaScript is single-threaded, but it can still perform asynchronous operations. It achieves this using the Event Loop together with browser or Node.js APIs, not by creating a new JavaScript thread for every async task.

Interview Tip

If asked this question, don't stop at 'blocking vs non-blocking.' Mention that JavaScript is single-threaded and asynchronous behavior is made possible by the Event Loop, Web APIs, and the Callback/Microtask Queues. This demonstrates a deeper understanding.

Interview Answer

"Synchronous programming executes code sequentially, where each statement blocks the next until it completes. Asynchronous programming allows long-running tasks like API calls and timers to execute without blocking the main thread. JavaScript achieves this using the Event Loop, Web APIs, and callback or microtask queues, while developers typically work with callbacks, Promises, or async/await."

question: Is JavaScript single-threaded or multi-threaded?
answer: JavaScript is single-threaded — one call stack, one thing at a time. It feels concurrent because of the event loop: async work is offloaded to the browser/Node APIs and their callbacks are queued back onto the stack when it's free. (Web Workers add real parallel threads, but they don't share the main thread.)
tip: One thread + event loop = the mental model for all async behaviour in JS.
deep:
Single-threaded, but concurrent

JavaScript has one call stack — it executes one statement at a time and can't run two pieces of JS in parallel on the main thread. What makes it feel concurrent is that slow work (timers, network, file I/O) is handed off to the host (browser or Node), which runs it elsewhere and queues a callback back when it's done.

Who does the async work?

The engine (V8) is single-threaded, but the runtime around it isn't. The browser provides Web APIs and a thread pool; Node uses libuv with its own worker threads. Your JS callback still runs back on the one main thread via the event loop.

console.log('start');
setTimeout(() => console.log('later'), 0); // offloaded to timer API
console.log('end');
// start, end, later

Why single-threaded is a deliberate choice

No shared-memory threads on the main thread means no data races or locks in your app code — simpler and safe for DOM manipulation. The cost: a long synchronous loop blocks everything (UI freezes), so you must keep the main thread free.

Getting real parallelism

  • Web Workers — separate threads for CPU-heavy work; communicate by messages, no shared DOM.
  • Node worker_threads — the Node equivalent.
  • SharedArrayBuffer / Atomics — actual shared memory between workers (advanced).

How to say it in an interview

“JavaScript itself is single-threaded — one call stack. It handles async work by offloading it to the browser or Node runtime and queuing the callbacks, which the event loop runs back on that single thread. For true parallelism I’d use Web Workers, which run on separate threads and communicate via messages.”

question: What is an event listener?
answer: An event listener is a function that waits for a specific event (such as a click, key press, or mouse movement) to occur on an HTML element. When that event happens, the function is automatically executed. Event listeners are usually attached using addEventListener().
tip: Prefer addEventListener() over onclick because it allows multiple listeners and can be removed later.
deep:
What is an Event?

An event is any action that occurs in the browser, either by the user or by the browser itself.

Common events include:

  • click – user clicks an element
  • dblclick – double click
  • input – input value changes
  • change – input loses focus after value changes
  • keydown – key is pressed
  • mouseover – mouse enters an element
  • submit – form is submitted

What is an Event Listener?

An event listener is a function that 'listens' for an event. When the specified event occurs, JavaScript automatically calls the listener function.

const button = document.getElementById('save');

button.addEventListener('click', () => {
  console.log('Button clicked');
});

Here:

  • button is the element.
  • click is the event.
  • The arrow function is the event listener (callback).

Syntax

element.addEventListener(eventName, callbackFunction);

Example:

const input = document.querySelector('input');

input.addEventListener('input', () => {
  console.log('Typing...');
});

Removing an Event Listener

You can remove an event listener using removeEventListener(). The same function reference must be passed.

function handleClick() {
  console.log('Clicked');
}

button.addEventListener('click', handleClick);

button.removeEventListener('click', handleClick);

addEventListener() vs onclick

addEventListener()onclick
Supports multiple listenersOnly one listener can be assigned
Can remove listenersNot easily removable
Supports capture and other optionsLimited functionality
button.onclick = () => console.log('First');
button.onclick = () => console.log('Second');
// Only 'Second' runs
button.addEventListener('click', () => console.log('First'));
button.addEventListener('click', () => console.log('Second'));
// Both listeners run

Common Events

EventDescription
clickUser clicks an element
dblclickUser double-clicks
inputInput value changes
changeValue changes and loses focus
keydownKeyboard key pressed
keyupKeyboard key released
mouseoverMouse enters an element
mouseoutMouse leaves an element
submitForm submitted

Common Interview Questions

  • Can multiple event listeners be attached to the same element? Yes, using addEventListener().
  • Why is addEventListener() preferred over onclick? Because it supports multiple listeners, can be removed, and provides additional options like capture and once.

How to say it in an interview

"An event listener is a function that waits for a specific event, such as a click or key press, to occur on an HTML element. When the event happens, the function is automatically executed. We usually attach event listeners using addEventListener() because it allows multiple listeners, supports additional options, and listeners can be removed using removeEventListener()."

question: slice() vs splice().
answer: slice() returns a new array without changing the original array. splice() modifies the original array by adding, removing, or replacing elements, and returns the removed elements.
tip: Remember: slice = copy, splice = change the original array.
deep:
What is slice()?

slice() creates a new array containing a portion of the original array. It does not modify the original array.

const numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50];

const result = numbers.slice(1, 4);

console.log(result);
// [20, 30, 40]

console.log(numbers);
// [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]

The first argument is the starting index (inclusive) and the second argument is the ending index (exclusive).

What is splice()?

splice() changes the original array. It can remove, insert, or replace elements.

const numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50];

const removed = numbers.splice(1, 2);

console.log(removed);
// [20, 30]

console.log(numbers);
// [10, 40, 50]

Adding Elements with splice()

const fruits = ['Apple', 'Orange'];

fruits.splice(1, 0, 'Banana');

console.log(fruits);
// ['Apple', 'Banana', 'Orange']

Replacing Elements with splice()

const colors = ['Red', 'Green', 'Blue'];

colors.splice(1, 1, 'Yellow');

console.log(colors);
// ['Red', 'Yellow', 'Blue']

Comparison

slice()splice()
Returns a new arrayModifies the original array
Does not mutateMutates the array
Used for copying a portionUsed for adding, removing, or replacing elements
Original array remains unchangedOriginal array is updated

Common Interview Questions

  • Which method mutates the original array? splice().
  • Which method returns a new array? slice().
  • Can splice() insert elements? Yes.
  • Can splice() replace elements? Yes.

How to say it in an interview

"The main difference is that slice() does not modify the original array—it returns a new array containing the selected elements. splice() modifies the original array and is used to add, remove, or replace elements. If I only need a copy, I use slice(). If I need to update the array itself, I use splice()."

question: call() vs apply() vs bind().
answer: All set this explicitly. call invokes immediately with arguments listed individually; apply invokes immediately with arguments as an array; bind returns a new function with this permanently bound (call it later).
tip: call/apply invoke now; bind returns a function to invoke later. apply takes an Array.
deep:
All three set this — they differ in when and how args are passed

MethodInvokesArguments
callimmediatelylisted individually
applyimmediatelyas one array
bindlater (returns a new fn)listed; can pre-fill some
function greet(g, p) { return `${g}, ${this.name}${p}`; }
const user = { name: 'Asha' };

greet.call(user, 'Hi', '!');       // 'Hi, Asha!'
greet.apply(user, ['Hi', '!']);    // 'Hi, Asha!'
const g = greet.bind(user);        // new function
g('Hi', '!');                      // 'Hi, Asha!'

Mnemonic: Apply = Array. Call = comma-separated. Bind = build-for-later.

bind also does partial application

function multiply(a, b) { return a * b; }
const double = multiply.bind(null, 2);
double(5); // 10  — first arg pre-filled

Real uses

  • Fix a lost this when passing a method as a callback: btn.addEventListener('click', this.handle.bind(this)).
  • Borrow methods: Array.prototype.slice.call(arguments) to arrayify array-likes.
  • Spread into a variadic fn: Math.max.apply(null, nums) (modern: Math.max(...nums)).

Caveats

You can't rebind an arrow function's this (it has none). And bind is permanent — binding an already-bound function again has no effect.

How to say it in an interview

“All three explicitly set this. call and apply invoke immediately — call takes comma-separated args, apply takes an array. bind returns a new function with this permanently fixed to call later, and can pre-fill arguments for partial application.”

question: What are template literals?
answer: Template literals are strings enclosed in backticks (`) instead of quotes. They allow you to embed variables and expressions using ${...} and create multi-line strings without using string concatenation.
tip: Use backticks (`) whenever you need variable interpolation or multi-line strings.
deep:
What are Template Literals?

Template literals are a feature introduced in ES6 that allow you to create strings using backticks (`) instead of single (') or double (") quotes.

const name = 'John';

const message = `Hello ${name}`;

console.log(message);
// Hello John

String Interpolation

The biggest advantage of template literals is string interpolation, which allows variables or JavaScript expressions to be embedded directly inside a string using ${...}.

const name = 'John';
const age = 25;

console.log(`Name: ${name}`);
console.log(`Age next year: ${age + 1}`);

Without template literals, you would use string concatenation.

// Old way
const message = 'Hello ' + name + ', you are ' + age + ' years old.';

// Modern way
const message = `Hello ${name}, you are ${age} years old.`;

Multi-line Strings

Template literals make writing multi-line strings much easier.

const text = `This is line 1
This is line 2
This is line 3`;

Previously, this required newline characters (\n) or string concatenation.

Using Expressions

Any valid JavaScript expression can be placed inside ${...}.

const a = 10;
const b = 20;

console.log(`${a} + ${b} = ${a + b}`);
// 10 + 20 = 30
const user = {
  firstName: 'John',
  lastName: 'Doe'
};

console.log(`Hello ${user.firstName} ${user.lastName}`);

Tagged Template Literals

Template literals can also be passed to a function before they are converted into a string. This is called a tagged template literal.

function highlight(strings, value) {
  return strings[0] + '<b>' + value + '</b>' + strings[1];
}

const name = 'John';

console.log(highlight`Hello ${name}`);
// Hello John

Tagged templates are commonly used in libraries like styled-components, localization (i18n), and HTML sanitization.

Template Literals vs String Concatenation

Template LiteralsString Concatenation
Uses backticks (`)Uses single or double quotes
Supports ${} interpolationUses + operator
Supports multi-line stringsRequires \n or concatenation
More readableCan become difficult to read

Common Interview Questions

  • What characters are used for template literals? Backticks (`), not single or double quotes.
  • Can you execute expressions inside a template literal? Yes, any valid JavaScript expression can be placed inside ${...}.
  • What is a tagged template literal? A template literal that is processed by a function before producing the final string.

How to say it in an interview

"Template literals are strings enclosed in backticks. They allow string interpolation using ${...}, support multi-line strings without using \n, and can evaluate JavaScript expressions directly inside the string. They are cleaner and more readable than traditional string concatenation."

question: How does garbage collection work in JavaScript?
answer: JavaScript uses automatic garbage collection (GC) to reclaim memory occupied by objects that are no longer reachable. Modern JavaScript engines (such as V8) primarily use the Mark-and-Sweep algorithm. Developers cannot manually free memory, but they can prevent memory leaks by removing unnecessary references, clearing timers, and unregistering event listeners.
tip: Remember: Reachable objects stay alive. Unreachable objects are automatically removed by the Garbage Collector.
deep:
What is Garbage Collection?

Garbage Collection (GC) is JavaScript's automatic memory management mechanism. When objects are no longer accessible from the application, the JavaScript engine automatically reclaims their memory.

Unlike languages such as C or C++, developers do not manually allocate or free memory.

Reachability

The most important concept in garbage collection is reachability.

An object is considered reachable if it can be accessed from one of the GC roots.

Common GC roots include:

  • Global variables.
  • Variables in the current Call Stack.
  • Active function parameters.
  • Closures that still reference variables.
let user = {
  name: 'John'
};

user = null;

After assigning null, no references remain to the original object, making it eligible for garbage collection.

Mark-and-Sweep Algorithm

Modern JavaScript engines such as Google's V8 use the Mark-and-Sweep algorithm.

  1. Start from the GC roots.
  2. Mark every reachable object.
  3. Traverse all referenced objects recursively.
  4. Objects that are not marked are unreachable.
  5. Sweep (delete) all unmarked objects and reclaim their memory.
Global Object
      │
      ▼
    user
      │
      ▼
  address
      │
      ▼
   city

(All marked ✓)

Unreachable object
      ▼
   oldData

(Not marked → Garbage Collected)

Circular References

Older reference-counting algorithms failed to clean circular references. Mark-and-Sweep handles them correctly.

let a = {};
let b = {};

a.ref = b;
b.ref = a;

a = null;
b = null;

Although the objects reference each other, neither is reachable from a GC root, so both are collected.

Common Causes of Memory Leaks

  • Global variables that are never cleared.
  • Forgotten setInterval() or setTimeout().
  • Event listeners that are never removed.
  • Detached DOM elements still referenced in JavaScript.
  • Large caches that keep growing.
  • Closures unintentionally holding large objects in memory.
// ❌ Memory Leak
const timer = setInterval(() => {
  console.log('Running');
}, 1000);

// ✅ Cleanup
clearInterval(timer);
// ❌ Event Listener Leak
button.addEventListener('click', handler);

// ✅ Cleanup
button.removeEventListener('click', handler);

Closures and Garbage Collection

A closure keeps variables alive as long as the closure itself is reachable.

function createCounter() {
  let count = 0;

  return () => ++count;
}

const counter = createCounter();

The variable count is not garbage collected because the returned function still references it.

Garbage Collection in Browsers

Modern JavaScript engines (V8, SpiderMonkey, JavaScriptCore) optimize garbage collection using techniques such as:

  • Generational Garbage Collection.
  • Incremental Garbage Collection.
  • Concurrent Garbage Collection.
  • Compacting memory to reduce fragmentation.

These optimizations reduce application pauses while cleaning memory.

Best Practices

  • Set unused objects to null when appropriate.
  • Clear timers using clearTimeout() and clearInterval().
  • Remove event listeners when components are destroyed.
  • Avoid unnecessary global variables.
  • Limit the size of caches.
  • Be mindful of closures retaining large objects.

Angular Example

In Angular, memory leaks commonly occur when Observables or subscriptions are not cleaned up.

private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();

this.userService.getUsers()
  .pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$))
  .subscribe();

ngOnDestroy() {
  this.destroy$.next();
  this.destroy$.complete();
}

This ensures subscriptions are released when the component is destroyed.

Interview Tip

Interviewers often ask whether developers can manually trigger garbage collection. The correct answer is No. JavaScript engines automatically decide when to run GC. Developers focus on preventing memory leaks by removing references, clearing timers, unsubscribing from Observables, and cleaning up event listeners.

Interview Answer

"JavaScript automatically manages memory using Garbage Collection. Modern engines use the Mark-and-Sweep algorithm, starting from GC roots and removing objects that are no longer reachable. Developers cannot manually free memory, but they should prevent memory leaks by clearing timers, removing event listeners, unsubscribing from Observables, avoiding unnecessary global variables, and releasing unused references."

question: How do you add or access properties dynamically on an object?
answer: Use bracket notation when the key is dynamic or not a valid identifier: obj[key] = value. Dot notation (obj.key) only works for fixed, valid names.
tip: Bracket notation is how you set a property whose name is in a variable.

question: for...of vs for...in.
answer: for...of loops through the values of an iterable like an Array, String, Map, or Set. for...in loops through the keys (property names) of an object. Use for...of for arrays and for...in for objects.
tip: Remember: for...of → values, for...in → keys. Avoid using for...in with arrays.
deep:
for...of

for...of is used to iterate over the values of an iterable such as an Array, String, Map, or Set.

const fruits = ['Apple', 'Banana', 'Orange'];

for (const fruit of fruits) {
  console.log(fruit);
}

// Apple
// Banana
// Orange

Notice that each iteration gives you the actual value.

const name = 'John';

for (const ch of name) {
  console.log(ch);
}

// J
// o
// h
// n

for...of also works with Map and Set.

const set = new Set([10, 20, 30]);

for (const value of set) {
  console.log(value);
}

for...in

for...in is used to iterate over the keys (property names) of an object.

const user = {
  name: 'John',
  age: 25,
  city: 'London'
};

for (const key in user) {
  console.log(key, user[key]);
}

// name John
// age 25
// city London

Here, the loop variable contains the property name (name, age, etc.), and you use object[key] to access the corresponding value.

What happens with Arrays?

Although for...in works on arrays, it returns the indexes, not the values.

const fruits = ['Apple', 'Banana', 'Orange'];

for (const index in fruits) {
  console.log(index);
}

// 0
// 1
// 2

To get the values, you would need:

for (const index in fruits) {
  console.log(fruits[index]);
}

This is why for...of is preferred for arrays.

for...of vs for...in

for...offor...in
Loops over valuesLoops over keys (property names)
Best for Arrays, Strings, Maps, and SetsBest for Objects
Returns actual valuesReturns property names or array indexes
Does not iterate inherited enumerable propertiesCan iterate inherited enumerable properties

Example Comparison

const numbers = [10, 20, 30];

// for...of
for (const value of numbers) {
  console.log(value);
}

// Output:
// 10
// 20
// 30

// for...in
for (const index in numbers) {
  console.log(index);
}

// Output:
// 0
// 1
// 2

When should you use each?

  • Use for...of when you need the values of an array, string, Map, or Set.
  • Use for...in when you need the property names (keys) of an object.

Common Interview Questions

  • Can for...in be used on arrays? Yes, but it returns indexes instead of values, so it is generally not recommended.
  • Which loop is recommended for arrays? for...of.
  • Which loop is recommended for objects? for...in.

How to say it in an interview

"I use for...of when I want to iterate over the values of an iterable like an array or string. I use for...in when I need to iterate over the keys of an object. Although for...in works on arrays, it returns indexes rather than values, so for...of is the preferred choice for arrays."

question: setTimeout() vs setInterval().
answer: setTimeout() executes a function once after a specified delay. setInterval() executes a function repeatedly at a fixed time interval until it is stopped.
tip: Use clearTimeout() to cancel a timeout and clearInterval() to stop an interval.

question: What is variable shadowing?
answer: When a variable declared in an inner scope has the same name as one in an outer scope, the inner one shadows (hides) the outer within that block. Common with function parameters and block-scoped let/const.
tip: Illegal shadowing: you can't shadow a let with a var in the same scope.
deep:
What it is

Shadowing happens when a variable in an inner scope has the same name as one in an enclosing scope. Inside the inner scope, the name resolves to the inner variable — the outer one is hidden (not overwritten).

let x = 1;
function f() {
  let x = 2;   // shadows the outer x
  return x;    // 2
}
f();           // 2
x;             // 1 — outer untouched

It follows the scope chain

JS resolves a name by walking outward: current block → enclosing function → … → global. The nearest declaration wins, so the inner one shadows the rest.

const v = 'global';
{
  const v = 'block';
  console.log(v); // 'block'
}
console.log(v);   // 'global'

Illegal shadowing

You can shadow across nested scopes, but you can't create a conflicting redeclaration in the same scope — mixing let and var for the same name throws:

function bad() {
  let a = 1;
  { var a = 2; } // ❌ SyntaxError: 'a' already declared
}

The loop-variable case

for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) { /* each iteration: a fresh i */ }

let in a loop head effectively creates a new binding per iteration — a form of scoping that shadows across iterations and fixes the classic closure-in-loop bug.

Should you do it?

Deliberate shadowing (e.g. narrowing a param inside a block) is fine and common. Accidental shadowing hides bugs — a linter rule (no-shadow) catches unintended cases.

How to say it in an interview

“Shadowing is when an inner-scope variable reuses an outer variable’s name and hides it within that scope — name resolution picks the nearest one. The outer value is untouched. It’s legal across nested scopes but not as a conflicting redeclaration in the same scope.”

question: Generator function vs normal function.
answer: A normal function runs from start to finish and returns a single value. A generator function (written using function*) can pause its execution using yield and continue later. Instead of returning a value directly, it returns an iterator that produces values one at a time.
tip: Remember: return ends a normal function, while yield pauses a generator and resumes when next() is called.
deep:
What is a Normal Function?

A normal function executes completely from start to finish. Once it reaches return, it finishes and cannot resume.

function greet() {
  console.log('Hello');
  return 'Done';
}

console.log(greet());

What is a Generator Function?

A generator function is declared using function*. Instead of returning one value, it can pause its execution using yield and continue later.

function* numbers() {
  yield 1;
  yield 2;
  yield 3;
}

Calling a generator does not execute its body immediately. It returns an iterator.

const iterator = numbers();

The function starts executing only when next() is called.

console.log(iterator.next());
// { value: 1, done: false }

console.log(iterator.next());
// { value: 2, done: false }

console.log(iterator.next());
// { value: 3, done: false }

console.log(iterator.next());
// { value: undefined, done: true }

yield vs return

return finishes a function completely, whereas yield pauses execution and remembers the current state until next() is called again.

Why use Generators?

  • Generate values lazily (only when needed).
  • Represent infinite sequences.
  • Create custom iterators.
  • Reduce memory usage for large datasets.
function* evenNumbers() {
  let n = 0;

  while (true) {
    yield n;
    n += 2;
  }
}

const even = evenNumbers();

console.log(even.next().value); // 0
console.log(even.next().value); // 2
console.log(even.next().value); // 4

Comparison

Normal FunctionGenerator Function
Runs completelyCan pause and resume
Uses returnUses yield
Returns a valueReturns an iterator
Cannot resume after finishingResumes with next()
Eager executionLazy execution

Common Interview Questions

  • What does a generator function return? An iterator.
  • What is the purpose of yield? It pauses execution and returns a value.
  • When are generators useful? For lazy evaluation, custom iterators, and generating large or infinite sequences.
  • Does calling a generator execute it immediately? No. Execution starts when next() is called.

How to say it in an interview

"A normal function executes from start to finish and returns a single value. A generator function uses yield to pause execution and returns an iterator. Each call to next() resumes the function from where it previously stopped, making generators useful for lazy evaluation and custom iteration."

question: Mutable vs immutable in JavaScript.
answer: Mutable values can be changed after they are created, while immutable values cannot. In JavaScript, primitive values (string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint) are immutable, whereas objects and arrays are mutable.
tip: Primitives create new values when modified. Objects and arrays can be changed directly.
deep:
What is Mutable?

A value is mutable if it can be changed after it is created.

const person = {
  name: 'John'
};

person.name = 'David';

console.log(person.name);
// David

The object itself was not replaced; only one of its properties changed.

const numbers = [1, 2];

numbers.push(3);

console.log(numbers);
// [1, 2, 3]

What is Immutable?

A value is immutable if it cannot be changed after it is created. Instead, any operation creates a new value.

let text = 'hello';

let upper = text.toUpperCase();

console.log(text);  // hello
console.log(upper); // HELLO

The original string remains unchanged because strings are immutable.

Primitive Types are Immutable

These JavaScript primitive types are immutable:

  • String
  • Number
  • Boolean
  • Null
  • Undefined
  • Symbol
  • BigInt

Objects and Arrays are Mutable

Objects and arrays can be modified without creating a new object.

const user = {
  name: 'John'
};

user.age = 25;

console.log(user);
// { name: 'John', age: 25 }
const fruits = ['Apple'];

fruits.push('Banana');

console.log(fruits);
// ['Apple', 'Banana']

Object.freeze()

Object.freeze() prevents modifications to an object's top-level properties.

const user = Object.freeze({
  name: 'John'
});

user.name = 'David';

console.log(user.name);
// John

Note that Object.freeze() is shallow. Nested objects can still be modified.

Mutable vs Immutable

MutableImmutable
Can be changed after creationCannot be changed after creation
Objects and ArraysPrimitive values
Changes happen in placeOperations return a new value

Common Interview Questions

  • Are arrays mutable? Yes.
  • Are objects mutable? Yes.
  • Are strings mutable? No.
  • Does Object.freeze() make nested objects immutable? No. It only freezes the top level.

How to say it in an interview

"Mutable values can be changed after they are created, while immutable values cannot. In JavaScript, all primitive types are immutable, whereas objects and arrays are mutable. Methods like push() modify arrays directly, while string methods such as toUpperCase() return a new string without changing the original."

question: What are truthy and falsy values?
answer: In a boolean context (if, &&, !), every value coerces to true or false. There are exactly 8 falsy values: false, 0, -0, 0n, "", null, undefined, and NaN. Everything else is truthy — including "0", "false", [], and {}.
tip: Empty array [] and empty object {} are TRUTHY — the classic gotcha.
code:
if ([]) console.log('runs');   // [] is truthy!
Boolean('0');  // true
Boolean(0);    // false
Boolean(NaN);  // false
deep:
The 8 falsy values (memorise these)

false
0    -0    0n        // zero, incl. BigInt
""                  // empty string
null
undefined
NaN

Anything not in that list is truthy. The surprising ones: "0", " ", "false", [], {}, and any function are all truthy.

Where it bites

if (arr.length) { }        // ✅ 0 is falsy, so empty array skips
if (arr) { }               // ❌ [] is truthy, always runs

if (count) { }             // ❌ skips when count === 0 (valid!)
if (count != null) { }     // ✅ only skips null/undefined

Short-circuiting uses this

const name = input || 'Guest';   // '' is falsy → 'Guest'
const n = value ?? 0;            // ?? only catches null/undefined, keeps 0/''

Prefer ?? over || when 0 or "" are valid values you don't want replaced.

How to say it in an interview

“There are exactly eight falsy values — false, 0, -0, 0n, "", null, undefined, NaN — and everything else is truthy, including empty arrays and objects. So I check arr.length, not the array itself.”

question: What does "use strict" do?
answer: 'use strict' enables Strict Mode, which makes JavaScript more secure by preventing common mistakes. For example, it throws an error if you use an undeclared variable, disallows duplicate parameter names, and changes some unsafe JavaScript behaviors into errors.
tip: ES Modules and JavaScript classes automatically run in Strict Mode, so you usually don't need to write 'use strict' manually.
deep:
What is Strict Mode?

Strict Mode is a safer version of JavaScript that helps catch common programming mistakes. It is enabled by placing 'use strict'; at the top of a JavaScript file or function.

'use strict';

console.log('Strict mode enabled');

Why use Strict Mode?

Strict Mode helps developers write cleaner and more reliable code by converting silent mistakes into errors.

1. Prevents Accidental Global Variables

Without Strict Mode, assigning a value to an undeclared variable creates a global variable.

// Without strict mode
x = 10;
console.log(x); // Works (creates global variable)

With Strict Mode:

'use strict';

x = 10;
// ReferenceError: x is not defined

2. Disallows Duplicate Parameter Names

'use strict';

function add(a, a) {
  return a;
}
// SyntaxError

3. Changes the Value of this

In a normal function call, this becomes undefined instead of the global object.

'use strict';

function show() {
  console.log(this);
}

show();
// undefined

Without Strict Mode, this would refer to the global object (window in browsers).

4. Prevents Deleting Variables

'use strict';

let x = 10;
delete x;
// SyntaxError

Where can you use it?

You can enable Strict Mode for an entire file or for a specific function.

// Entire file
'use strict';

function test() {
  console.log('Hello');
}
function test() {
  'use strict';

  console.log('Strict mode only inside this function');
}

Do you still need it?

In modern JavaScript, ES Modules (files using import or export) and class bodies automatically run in Strict Mode. Therefore, you rarely need to add 'use strict'; manually in modern applications.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is the purpose of Strict Mode? It helps catch common JavaScript mistakes by turning them into errors.
  • Does Angular/React need 'use strict'? Usually no, because modern build tools generate ES Modules, which are already in Strict Mode.
  • Can Strict Mode be enabled inside a function? Yes, it can apply to an individual function instead of the entire file.

How to say it in an interview

"Strict Mode is a safer version of JavaScript that catches common mistakes such as accidental global variables, duplicate parameter names, and unsafe uses of this. It is enabled using 'use strict';, although in modern JavaScript, ES Modules and classes already run in Strict Mode automatically."

question: find, filter, some, every, includes — what do they do?
answer:
• filter — returns a new array of all items passing a test.
  • find — returns the first matching item (or undefined).
  • findIndex — the index of the first match (or -1).
  • some — true if at least one item passes.
  • every — true if all items pass.
  • includes — true if the array contains a value (uses ===).
tip: find returns the item; filter returns an array. Use find when you expect one result.
code:
const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4];
nums.filter(n => n % 2 === 0); // [2, 4]
nums.find(n => n > 2);        // 3
nums.some(n => n > 3);        // true
nums.every(n => n > 0);       // true
nums.includes(2);             // true

question: What is an Execution Context in JavaScript?
answer: An Execution Context is the environment in which JavaScript code runs. It contains information about variables, functions, the value of this, and how the code should execute. Every JavaScript program starts with a Global Execution Context, and every function call creates a new Function Execution Context.
tip: Remember: Every function call creates a new Execution Context. JavaScript manages these contexts using the Call Stack.
deep:
What is an Execution Context?

An Execution Context is the environment where JavaScript code is executed. Whenever JavaScript runs a script or calls a function, it creates an execution context that stores everything needed to execute that code.

It contains:

  • Variables
  • Functions
  • The value of this
  • The scope information

Think of an execution context as a workspace created by JavaScript before executing code.

Types of Execution Context

  • Global Execution Context (GEC) - Created once when the JavaScript program starts.
  • Function Execution Context (FEC) - Created every time a function is called.
  • Eval Execution Context - Created only when using eval(). Rarely used.

1. Global Execution Context

When JavaScript starts executing a file, it first creates the Global Execution Context.

let a = 10;

function greet() {
  console.log('Hello');
}

The Global Execution Context stores:

  • a
  • greet()
  • Global this

Only one Global Execution Context exists for a JavaScript program.

2. Function Execution Context

Whenever a function is called, JavaScript creates a brand-new Function Execution Context.

function add(a, b) {
  const result = a + b;
  return result;
}

add(10, 20);

Calling add() creates a new execution context containing:

  • Parameters (a, b)
  • Local variables (result)
  • The function's this

After the function finishes, its execution context is removed from memory.

How is an Execution Context Created?

Every execution context is created in two phases.

1. Creation Phase (Memory Creation Phase)

Before executing any code, JavaScript scans the code and allocates memory.

  • Variables declared with var are initialized with undefined.
  • let and const are hoisted but remain in the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ).
  • Function declarations are stored completely in memory.
  • The value of this is determined.
console.log(a);
foo();

var a = 10;

function foo() {
  console.log('Hello');
}

Memory after the Creation Phase:

a → undefined
foo → function
this → global object (or undefined in modules)
2. Execution Phase

JavaScript now executes the code line by line.

  • Assignments happen.
  • Expressions are evaluated.
  • Functions are executed.
var a = 10;

Now the value of a changes from undefined to 10.

The Call Stack

JavaScript uses a Call Stack to manage execution contexts.

function one() {
  two();
}

function two() {
  three();
}

function three() {
  console.log('Done');
}

one();

Call Stack:

Start

Global Execution Context
        ↓
one()
        ↓
two()
        ↓
three()
        ↓
console.log()

When three() finishes, it is removed from the stack.

Global
 ↓
one()
 ↓
two()

Eventually only the Global Execution Context remains.

Execution Context Lifecycle

Create Execution Context
          ↓
Creation Phase
          ↓
Execution Phase
          ↓
Function Returns
          ↓
Execution Context Destroyed

Execution Context vs Call Stack

Execution ContextCall Stack
Execution environmentStores execution contexts
Contains variables and functionsTracks function calls
Created for every functionPushes and pops contexts
Destroyed after executionAlways executes the top context

Real-world Example

function login(user) {
  const token = generateToken(user);
  saveToken(token);
}

function generateToken(user) {
  return user + '-123';
}

function saveToken(token) {
  console.log(token);
}

login('john');

The execution order is:

  1. Create Global Execution Context.
  2. Create login() context.
  3. Create generateToken() context.
  4. Destroy generateToken().
  5. Create saveToken().
  6. Destroy saveToken().
  7. Destroy login().
  8. Global Execution Context remains until the program ends.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is an Execution Context? It is the environment in which JavaScript code executes.
  • How many Global Execution Contexts exist? Only one.
  • When is a Function Execution Context created? Every time a function is invoked.
  • What are the two phases of an Execution Context? Creation Phase and Execution Phase.
  • What data is stored inside an Execution Context? Variables, functions, scope information, and the value of this.
  • How does the Call Stack relate to Execution Context? The Call Stack stores and manages all execution contexts.

Execution Context vs Lexical Scope

Execution ContextLexical Scope
Created when code executes.Determined when code is written.
Stores variables and execution state.Determines where variables can be accessed.
Managed by the Call Stack.Used to resolve variable lookup.

How to say it in an interview

"Execution Context is the environment in which JavaScript executes code. Every program begins with one Global Execution Context, and every function call creates a new Function Execution Context. Each context is created in two phases: the Creation Phase, where memory is allocated for variables and functions, and the Execution Phase, where code runs line by line. JavaScript manages all execution contexts using the Call Stack."

question: What is Lexical Scope?
answer: Lexical Scope means JavaScript determines where variables are accessible based on where functions and variables are written in the code, not where they are called. An inner function can access variables from its own scope as well as all outer scopes. This behavior is known as the scope chain and is the foundation of closures.
tip: Remember: Lexical Scope is decided when the code is written, not when it runs. Closures work because of Lexical Scope.
deep:
What is Lexical Scope?

Lexical Scope means the accessibility of variables is determined by the physical location of functions and variables in the source code.

JavaScript uses Lexical (Static) Scoping, which means the scope of a variable is fixed when the code is written.

const a = 10;

function outer() {
  const b = 20;

  function inner() {
    console.log(a);
    console.log(b);
  }

  inner();
}

outer();

inner() can access both a and b because they are in its outer lexical scopes.

Types of Scope

  • Global Scope - Accessible everywhere.
  • Function Scope - Accessible only inside a function.
  • Block Scope - Accessible only inside a block ({ }) when using let or const.

1. Global Scope

const company = 'Google';

function show() {
  console.log(company);
}

show();

Variables declared outside every function belong to the Global Scope.

2. Function Scope

function greet() {
  const message = 'Hello';
  console.log(message);
}

greet();

console.log(message); // ReferenceError

Variables declared with var, let, or const inside a function are available only within that function.

3. Block Scope

if (true) {
  let age = 25;
}

console.log(age); // ReferenceError

let and const are block-scoped, while var is not.

if (true) {
  var x = 10;
}

console.log(x); // 10

Nested Scope

Functions can be nested inside other functions. Inner functions automatically inherit access to variables declared in outer functions.

function outer() {
  const city = 'Bangalore';

  function middle() {
    const state = 'Karnataka';

    function inner() {
      console.log(city);
      console.log(state);
    }

    inner();
  }

  middle();
}

The innermost function has access to every outer scope.

The Scope Chain

When JavaScript cannot find a variable in the current scope, it searches the next outer scope. This process continues until the variable is found or the Global Scope is reached.

const country = 'India';

function outer() {
  const state = 'Odisha';

  function inner() {
    console.log(country);
    console.log(state);
  }

  inner();
}
inner()
   ↓
outer()
   ↓
Global Scope
   ↓
Not Found → ReferenceError

Lexical Scope vs Dynamic Scope

JavaScript uses Lexical Scope, not Dynamic Scope.

const name = 'John';

function printName() {
  console.log(name);
}

function execute() {
  const name = 'David';
  printName();
}

execute();

Output:

John

The output is John because printName() uses the scope where it was defined, not where it was called.

Relationship with Closures

Closures exist because of Lexical Scope.

function counter() {
  let count = 0;

  return function () {
    count++;
    console.log(count);
  };
}

const increment = counter();
increment();
increment();

The returned function remembers the lexical environment where it was created.

Lexical Scope vs Execution Context

Lexical ScopeExecution Context
Determined when code is written.Created when code executes.
Controls variable access.Stores variables and execution information.
Never changes.Created and destroyed during execution.

Real-world Example

const apiUrl = 'https://api.example.com';

function getUsers() {
  fetch(apiUrl + '/users');
}

function getOrders() {
  fetch(apiUrl + '/orders');
}

Both functions access apiUrl through Lexical Scope.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is Lexical Scope? Variable accessibility determined by where code is written.
  • What is the Scope Chain? JavaScript searches outer scopes until it finds a variable.
  • Does JavaScript use Lexical Scope or Dynamic Scope? Lexical Scope.
  • Why do Closures work? Because functions remember their lexical environment.
  • Can an outer function access variables of an inner function? No.
  • Can an inner function access variables of an outer function? Yes.

Common Mistake

function outer() {
  const x = 10;
}

console.log(x); // ReferenceError

The outer scope cannot access variables declared inside an inner scope.

How to say it in an interview

"Lexical Scope means JavaScript determines variable accessibility based on where functions are written in the source code. Inner functions can access variables from their own scope and all outer scopes through the scope chain. This behavior is fixed when the code is written and is the reason closures work in JavaScript."

question: What is the difference between localStorage, sessionStorage, and Cookies?
answer: localStorage, sessionStorage, and Cookies are browser storage mechanisms used to store data on the client side. localStorage stores data permanently until it is manually removed, sessionStorage stores data only for the current browser tab, while Cookies are small pieces of data that can automatically be sent to the server with every HTTP request.
tip: Use localStorage for user preferences, sessionStorage for temporary session data, and Cookies only when the server needs the stored information.
deep:
What are Browser Storage APIs?

Modern browsers provide multiple ways to store data on the client side. The three most commonly used are localStorage, sessionStorage, and Cookies.

Although all three store data inside the browser, they differ in lifetime, storage size, and whether the data is automatically sent to the server.

1. localStorage

localStorage stores data with no expiration date. The data remains available even after closing and reopening the browser until it is manually removed.

localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark');

const theme = localStorage.getItem('theme');
console.log(theme);

localStorage.removeItem('theme');

localStorage.clear();

Common uses:

  • Dark/Light theme
  • Language preference
  • Remember user settings
  • Recently viewed items

2. sessionStorage

sessionStorage stores data only for the current browser tab.

Once the tab or browser window is closed, the data is automatically deleted.

sessionStorage.setItem('username', 'John');

console.log(sessionStorage.getItem('username'));

Common uses:

  • Multi-step forms
  • Temporary filters
  • Wizard progress
  • Current session data

3. Cookies

Cookies are small pieces of data stored by the browser that can automatically be sent to the server with every HTTP request.

document.cookie = 'username=John';

document.cookie = 'theme=dark; max-age=3600';

Cookies are commonly used for:

  • Authentication
  • Session management
  • User tracking
  • Remember Me functionality

Comparison Table

FeaturelocalStoragesessionStorageCookies
Storage Size~5-10 MB~5 MB~4 KB
ExpirationUntil manually removedUntil tab closesConfigurable
Accessible from JavaScriptYesYesYes (unless HttpOnly)
Automatically sent to serverNoNoYes
Best forUser preferencesTemporary session dataAuthentication & sessions

Storage APIs

localStorage
localStorage.setItem('name', 'John');

const value = localStorage.getItem('name');

localStorage.removeItem('name');

localStorage.clear();
sessionStorage
sessionStorage.setItem('step', '2');

const step = sessionStorage.getItem('step');

sessionStorage.removeItem('step');

Storing Objects

Storage APIs only store strings. Objects must be converted using JSON.stringify().

const user = {
  id: 1,
  name: 'John'
};

localStorage.setItem('user', JSON.stringify(user));

const storedUser = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('user'));

console.log(storedUser.name);

Which One Should You Use?

RequirementRecommended Storage
Save theme preferencelocalStorage
Remember languagelocalStorage
Shopping cart during sessionsessionStorage
Multi-page formsessionStorage
User authenticationCookies (HttpOnly)
Remember loginCookies

Security Considerations

Never store sensitive information such as passwords in browser storage.

  • localStorage is vulnerable to XSS attacks because JavaScript can access it.
  • sessionStorage has the same limitation.
  • Cookies can be made safer using the HttpOnly and Secure flags.
Set-Cookie: token=abc123;
HttpOnly;
Secure;
SameSite=Strict;

With HttpOnly, JavaScript cannot access the cookie.

JWT Token Storage

One of the most common interview questions is where to store JWT tokens.

StorageRecommendation
localStorageEasy but vulnerable to XSS.
sessionStorageSafer than localStorage for temporary sessions.
HttpOnly CookieRecommended for authentication because JavaScript cannot access it.

Real-world Examples

// Save theme
localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark');

// Save selected language
localStorage.setItem('language', 'en');

// Save current form step
sessionStorage.setItem('currentStep', '3');

Common Interview Questions

  • What is the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage? localStorage persists until removed, while sessionStorage is cleared when the browser tab closes.
  • Which storage is automatically sent to the server? Cookies.
  • Can localStorage store objects? No. Objects must be converted using JSON.stringify().
  • Where should JWT tokens be stored? Prefer HttpOnly Cookies for better security.
  • Which storage has the largest capacity? localStorage.

How to say it in an interview

"localStorage, sessionStorage, and Cookies are browser storage mechanisms. localStorage stores data permanently until it is removed manually, sessionStorage stores data only for the current browser tab, and Cookies can be automatically sent to the server with every HTTP request. For user preferences, I use localStorage. For temporary session data, I use sessionStorage. For authentication, HttpOnly Cookies are generally the safest choice."
